It mirrors real ETL and analytics engineering workflows, documenting each transformation step for clarity, auditability, and reproducibility.
Raw → Cleaned → Canonicalised → Enriched → Feature‑Engineered → Model‑Ready
The analysis spans both logistics (transactional) and behavioural (web‑session) domains, integrating them into a unified analytical foundation. The notebook focuses on:
All logic is implemented inline to ensure full transparency and reproducibility, providing a consulting‑grade analytical workflow that bridges operational supply‑chain data with behavioural customer insights.

title: Supply Chain and Customer Behaviour Analytics: End‑to‑End EDA, Data Quality Assessment, and Behavioural and Logistics Feature Engineering
text:
Dataset Information
Source:
This notebook uses the SMART Supply Chain for Big Data Analysis dataset, publicly available on Mendeley Data.
Dataset link: https://data.mendeley.com/datasets/8gx2fvg2k6/5
Dataset Composition:
The dataset consists of three complementary files:

Structured Data — DataCoSupplyChainDataset.csv
A wide, denormalised transactional dataset containing customer attributes, product metadata, order‑level logistics information, and geographic fields.
Unstructured / Semi‑Structured Data — tokenized_access_logs.csv
A clickstream‑style behavioural dataset capturing page views, URL paths, product interactions, timestamps, and IP‑level activity.
Metadata / Documentation — DescriptionDataCoSupplyChain.csv
A descriptive file providing variable definitions and business context for the fields in the structured logistics dataset.

Together, these files support cross‑domain analytics spanning supply‑chain operations, customer behaviour, and web‑session activity.

title: Supply Chain and Customer Behaviour Analytics: End‑to‑End EDA, Data Quality Assessment, and Behavioural and Logistics Feature Engineering
text:
Loading Raw Data
The logistics, access‑logs, and dataset description files are loaded exactly as stored on disk, without any cleaning or transformation. This preserves full visibility into the original source structure before any processing.
The files use different encodings, and these choices are intentional and evidence‑based:

DescriptionDataCoSupplyChain.csv and DataCoSupplyChainDataset.csv contain extended ASCII characters originating from legacy export systems. Attempts to load them using UTF‑8 raise decoding errors, while Latin‑1 decodes all characters without loss or replacement. Latin‑1 is therefore the safest choice for preserving the raw text exactly as stored.
tokenized_access_logs.csv loads cleanly under UTF‑8 without decoding errors. The file contains URL‑encoded strings and standard ASCII/UTF‑8 characters, making UTF‑8 the correct and lossless choice.

Using the correct encoding for each file ensures that the raw datasets are ingested faithfully, without silent corruption, dropped characters, or unintended coercions. This step establishes a transparent and reproducible foundation for all downstream cleaning, mapping, and analysis.

Structured Logistics Data (Preview)
The DataCoSupplyChainDataset.csv file is a wide, denormalised table combining customer attributes, product metadata, order‑item facts, pricing fields, discount information, delivery status, and geographic attributes. title: Supply Chain and Customer Behaviour Analytics: End‑to‑End EDA, Data Quality Assessment, and Behavioural and Logistics Feature Engineering
text:
Inspection of Raw Data
Before applying any cleaning, mapping, or transformation steps, the raw logistics and access‑log datasets are inspected exactly as they exist on disk. This mirrors the Raw Layer of the ETL pipeline and establishes a transparent, auditable starting point for all downstream processing.
The Supply Chain dataset under investigation combines transactional logistics data (orders, products, customers, shipping details) with behavioural web access logs (page views, URL actions, timestamps, IP activity). These two sources ultimately feed into unified customer‑ and session‑level analytics, but they originate from different systems, formats, and export processes.
Inspecting the raw files in their unmodified form allows us to:

understand the original schema before any transformations,
view the raw column names as exported from the source systems,
examine raw dtypes, which often reveal mixed types, numeric‑as‑string issues, or date fields stored as plain text,
identify formatting inconsistencies such as whitespace, URL‑encoded strings, or irregular timestamp formats,
validate that the raw files match the dataset description provided by the vendor,
confirm that the chosen file encodings have preserved all characters without corruption,
justify the cleaning, mapping, and enrichment logic implemented later in the notebook.

This inspection step documents the true starting point of the data journey and ensures that the notebook remains fully transparent, reproducible, and aligned with the ETL and feature‑engineering workflow that follows.

Inspecting Dataset Description
The dataset description file is loaded exactly as provided by the vendor, without any cleaning or modification. This file documents the original field names, business definitions, and structural metadata associated with the Supply Chain dataset.
Inspecting the description file in its raw form allows us to:

verify that the fields listed in the documentation align with the columns present in the raw logistics export,
understand the intended meaning and business context of each attribute before applying transformations,
identify deprecated, unused, or ambiguous fields that may require clarification during cleaning,
cross‑reference the vendor’s schema with the actual raw data to detect inconsistencies or missing elements.

This step establishes a clear reference point for the subsequent cleaning and canonical mapping stages, ensuring that all transformations remain faithful to the dataset’s documented structure.

Inspection complete.
======================================================================


The raw logistics file is a large, denormalised export containing over 180k rows and 53 columns, combining customer attributes, product metadata, geographic coordinates, order‑level facts, and shipping details into a single table. The first rows show repeated product and customer information across multiple order items, confirming that the dataset is structured at the order‑item grain rather than at the order or customer level. Naming conventions remain inconsistent, with a mix of spaced names, underscored names, and irregular fields such as order date (DateOrders) and shipping date (DateOrders), indicating multiple upstream systems or export processes. Several fields contain masked values (e.g., customer email and password), and geographic fields include both coordinates and textual regions, suggesting heterogeneous source systems. The presence of negative profit values, mixed date formats stored as text, and missing values in fields like Order Zipcode and product descriptions highlights the need for careful type enforcement, cleaning, and validation before any modelling or aggregation.
The dtype profile confirms that the dataset arrives in a highly mixed and partially inconsistent state. Several fields that should be numeric identifiers (such as Customer Zipcode and Order Zipcode) are loaded as floats, indicating the presence of missing values or formatting issues that forced pandas to upcast them. All date fields (order date (DateOrders) and shipping date (DateOrders)) are stored as plain text rather than true datetime types, reinforcing the earlier observation that naming and formatting conventions vary across the export. Text-heavy fields such as customer names, product names, and geographic attributes are correctly typed as objects, but the presence of masked values (e.g., emails and passwords) suggests upstream anonymisation.
The missing‑value summary highlights several structural issues. Product Description is entirely empty, implying that the vendor either removed this field for privacy reasons or the source system never populated it. Order Zipcode is missing in more than 86% of rows, which limits its usefulness for geographic analysis unless supplemented or imputed. Minor gaps in Customer Lname and Customer Zipcode indicate occasional data-entry or export inconsistencies. Together, these patterns show that while the dataset is rich and detailed, it requires substantial type enforcement, column standardisation, and careful handling of sparsity before it can support reliable modelling or operational insights.

The access‑log file is a high‑volume behavioural dataset with nearly 470k rows but only 8 fields, indicating a narrow, event‑level structure focused on recording product‑view or browsing activity. The first rows show a consistent pattern: each record captures a single page access with the product name, its category, the timestamp of the visit, the hour extracted separately, the department being browsed, the visitor’s IP address, and the full URL path. This confirms that the dataset is structured at the web‑session event level rather than at the customer or order level.
All fields except Hour are loaded as text, including the Date column, which suggests that timestamps will require parsing before any temporal analysis. The url field contains encoded characters (e.g., %20), indicating that URL decoding will be necessary to extract clean product and category names. The ip field appears consistently populated and may support geolocation or sessionisation later, though it currently remains a raw string.
The missing‑value summary shows no nulls across any column, which is typical for system‑generated logs where each field is required for the event record. However, the absence of missingness does not imply cleanliness: category names, departments, and product names may still contain inconsistencies, mixed casing, or encoding artefacts. Overall, the dataset is structurally simple but semantically rich, and will require careful parsing, normalisation, and feature extraction before it can be aligned with the logistics dataset for behavioural analysis.

Both datasets contain inconsistent naming conventions, including: - spaces in column names, - underscores in some fields, - parentheses in date fields, - mixed casing, - and vendor‑specific patterns.

Interpretation of Product‑Name Matching Between fact_logistics_raw and fact_access_logs_raw
Sampling product names from fact_logistics_raw and checking whether they appear in fact_access_logs_raw reveals a clear structural mismatch between the two datasets. Only 72 out of 118 sampled products match exactly, and the remaining 46 products fail to match even after case‑insensitive comparison, which means the mismatch is not caused by casing differences. Instead, the non‑matching items fall into several meaningful categories that reflect deeper inconsistencies in how products are named, encoded, or represented across the two systems.
A large subset of unmatched items consists of truncated product names, where the vendor export in fact_logistics_raw appears to cut off long strings (e.g., "Glove It Women's Mod Oval 3-Zip Carry All Gol"). Others differ due to year‑based SKU variations, such as 2014 vs. 2017 versions of the same product line. Several unmatched items represent generic or placeholder categories (e.g., “Industrial consumer electronics”, “Sports Books”, “Toys”), which are unlikely to appear in browsing logs. A further group includes products with punctuation, spacing, or formatting differences, such as trailing spaces or apostrophes, which prevent direct string matching. Finally, some items appear only in logistics (purchased but not browsed) or only in access logs (browsed but never purchased), indicating natural behavioural differences rather than data quality issues.
Together, these patterns show that direct product‑name joins between the two datasets are unreliable in their raw form. The mismatches arise from structural differences in naming conventions, truncation, encoding, SKU versioning, and product taxonomy. This justifies the need for a structured product‑normalisation step before any integration or behavioural‑to‑transactional linkage. The inspection confirms that the next phase should focus on URL decoding, punctuation normalisation, token‑based comparison, and fuzzy matching to reconcile product identities across datasets.

Inspection Summary: Cross‑Dataset Product Name Structure
The inspection phase reveals that fact_logistics_raw and fact_access_logs_raw represent product names using fundamentally different structures, levels of detail, and naming conventions. Across URL‑decoded paths, punctuation‑stripped names, token‑overlap samples, and token‑overlap density checks, a consistent pattern emerges: raw product names cannot serve as reliable join keys between behavioural and transactional datasets. The mismatches are not random; they follow clear structural categories that justify a more robust reconciliation approach in the next phase.

Structural Differences in Product Naming
Inspection shows that fact_access_logs_raw preserves full, human‑readable product titles embedded within URL paths. These names include punctuation, multi‑word descriptors, model identifiers, and complete marketing language. In contrast, fact_logistics_raw contains a mixture of complete product names, truncated names that end mid‑word, and generic category‑level placeholders. This inconsistency means that even after case‑insensitive comparison, many logistics‑side names cannot match their access‑side counterparts.


Impact of Truncation and Encoding
Truncation is one of the most prominent structural issues in fact_logistics_raw. Many names end abruptly, losing key tokens that would otherwise align with access‑side names. URL decoding confirms that access‑side names retain punctuation and spacing that logistics‑side names often lack. These differences prevent direct string matching and highlight the need for later normalisation steps such as punctuation handling and token‑based comparison.


Presence of Non‑SKU and Placeholder Items
A significant subset of logistics‑side entries represent broad categories rather than specific SKUs (e.g., “industrial consumer electronics”, “sports books”, “dvds”). These items naturally have no counterpart in access logs, which only record browsed SKUs. This explains many of the zero‑overlap cases and confirms that some mismatches reflect genuine behavioural differences rather than data quality issues.


SKU Version and Model‑Year Variations
Inspection also reveals systematic differences in SKU versions. Logistics often contains older model years (e.g., 2014 variants), while access logs contain newer versions (e.g., 2017). These year‑based discrepancies create legitimate mismatches that cannot be resolved through simple string comparison.


Token‑Level Similarity Patterns
Token‑overlap inspection shows that when the same product exists in both datasets, the token sets align strongly—even when logistics names are truncated or punctuation differs. Partial overlaps reveal product‑family relationships, while zero‑overlap cases highlight placeholders or items not present on the browsed website. Token‑overlap density further confirms that popular product families generate high overlap counts, while niche or generic items produce low or zero overlap.


Implications for the Next Phase
The inspection confirms that mismatches arise from structural naming differences, truncation, encoding, SKU versioning, and the presence of non‑SKU placeholders. These findings justify moving into a structured reconciliation phase that incorporates URL decoding, punctuation normalisation, token‑based comparison, and fuzzy matching. These steps will be necessary to harmonise product identities before any cross‑dataset integration or behavioural‑to‑transactional linkage.



Event Density Checks
High‑level event density checks help identify: - traffic spikes, - missing periods, - batch‑generated logs, - and overall temporal coverage.
These checks remain part of raw inspection and do not modify the data.

Consolidated Inspection Summary
The inspection phase reveals that fact_access_logs_raw and fact_logistics_raw differ not only in content but in the fundamental structure of how products, categories, and customer interactions are represented. Across URL decoding, punctuation stripping, token‑overlap diagnostics, temporal distributions, and categorical cardinality checks, a consistent pattern emerges: the two datasets cannot be joined or reconciled using raw string fields alone. The mismatches are structural, systematic, and expected given the nature of the source systems.

Product Name Structure Across Datasets
Inspection of URL‑decoded paths shows that fact_access_logs_raw preserves full, human‑readable product names embedded within navigational URLs. These names include punctuation, multi‑word descriptors, model identifiers, and complete marketing language. In contrast, fact_logistics_raw contains a mixture of complete names, truncated names that end mid‑word, and generic category‑level placeholders. Punctuation‑stripped inspection confirms that truncation is widespread and systematic, with many logistics names losing key tokens required for direct alignment.
Token‑overlap diagnostics demonstrate that when the same product exists in both datasets, the token sets align strongly—even when logistics names are truncated or formatted differently. Partial overlaps reveal product‑family relationships, while zero‑overlap cases correspond to placeholders, offline‑only items, or SKUs not present in browsing logs. Token‑overlap density further shows that popular product families generate high overlap counts, while niche or generic items produce low or zero overlap. These patterns confirm that token‑level similarity is meaningful, whereas raw string equality is unreliable.


SKU Versioning and Model‑Year Differences
Several mismatches arise from SKU version differences. Logistics often contains older model years (e.g., 2014 variants), while access logs contain newer versions (e.g., 2017). These discrepancies are legitimate and reflect natural product‑lifecycle differences rather than data quality issues. They also reinforce that product identity cannot be inferred from raw names alone.


Presence of Non‑SKU and Placeholder Items
A significant subset of logistics entries represent broad categories rather than specific SKUs (e.g., “industrial consumer electronics”, “sports books”, “dvds”). These items naturally have no counterpart in access logs, which only record browsed SKUs. This explains many of the zero‑overlap cases and highlights that some mismatches reflect genuine behavioural differences rather than structural inconsistencies.


Temporal Patterns in Access Logs
Daily and hourly distributions show that access logs are heavily skewed toward a small number of high‑traffic days and peak sharply during daytime and evening hours. This confirms that browsing behaviour is bursty and time‑dependent. The temporal structure is consistent with real‑world retail traffic patterns and does not indicate data quality issues. It also suggests that any downstream behavioural modelling must account for strong temporal seasonality.


Categorical Cardinality Differences
Categorical cardinality checks reveal that fact_logistics_raw contains high‑cardinality customer attributes (e.g., thousands of unique cities, names, and streets) and a moderate number of product‑related categories (118 product names, 50 category names, 11 departments). In contrast, fact_access_logs_raw contains far fewer unique products (76), categories (33), and departments (6). This asymmetry confirms that the two datasets represent different slices of the product universe and that browsing behaviour does not cover the full transactional catalogue.


Implications for Data Integration
Across all inspection outputs, the evidence consistently shows that:

Raw product names cannot be used as join keys due to truncation, punctuation differences, SKU versioning, and placeholder items.
URL decoding and punctuation normalisation are necessary but insufficient on their own.
Token‑based comparison is robust to truncation and formatting differences and provides meaningful semantic signals.
Fuzzy matching will be required to reconcile near‑matches and truncated names.
Some products will never match due to behavioural differences or placeholder entries, and this is expected.

These findings justify transitioning into a structured cleaning and reconciliation phase that incorporates URL decoding, punctuation normalisation, token‑based comparison, and fuzzy matching to harmonise product identities before any cross‑dataset integration or behavioural‑to‑transactional linkage.



Dataset Grain Comparison
The two raw datasets operate at different levels of granularity:

fact_logistics_raw is structured at the order‑item level, where each row represents a purchased product within an order. Customer, product, and geographic attributes repeat across multiple rows.
fact_access_logs_raw is structured at the web‑event level, where each row represents a single product‑view or browsing action captured from the website logs.

This difference in grain is important for downstream modelling and integration. Any attempt to align behavioural activity with transactional outcomes will require careful aggregation, normalisation, and key‑mapping between these two levels.

title: Supply Chain and Customer Behaviour Analytics: End‑to‑End EDA, Data Quality Assessment, and Behavioural and Logistics Feature Engineering
text:
Bronze Layer

Data Cleaning and Canonicalisation Strategy
The inspection phase establishes that the raw datasets arrive in a highly inconsistent, partially truncated, and structurally heterogeneous state. The logistics dataset is a wide, denormalised order‑item table with mixed encodings, irregular column names, masked customer fields, inconsistent date formats, and substantial missingness in key geographic attributes. The access‑log dataset, while narrower and cleaner, contains URL‑encoded paths, mixed‑casing category labels, and behavioural product names that differ structurally from the transactional product catalogue.
Manual cross‑checking against Google Maps confirms an important semantic distinction in the logistics geography fields: Customer City, Customer Country, Customer State, Customer Street, Customer Zipcode, Latitude, and Longitude describe the store location where the customer made the purchase, whereas Market, Order City, Order Country, Order Region, and Order State describe the destination region where the order is delivered or shipped. This aligns with the dataset description and means that what appear to be duplicate geographic fields are in fact two separate geographic grains: point‑of‑sale location vs. delivery region. Any cleaning, renaming, or aggregation must preserve this distinction explicitly to avoid conflating store‑level and destination‑level geographies.
Across all inspection layers — raw schema review, data types and missingness profiling, URL decoding, punctuation stripping, token‑overlap diagnostics, temporal distribution checks, and categorical cardinality analysis — a consistent pattern emerges: the two datasets cannot be reliably analysed or integrated without a structured, multi‑stage cleaning and canonicalisation process. The issues observed are not isolated anomalies but systemic characteristics of the upstream export processes.
This cleaning phase therefore focuses on establishing a unified, analysis‑ready foundation by addressing the following evidence‑based requirements:

Column standardisation and semantic clarification to resolve inconsistent naming conventions (spaces, underscores, parentheses, mixed casing) and to encode the confirmed semantic split between store‑location fields and delivery‑region fields in the logistics dataset.
Type enforcement and date parsing to convert raw text fields into proper numeric, categorical, and datetime types, ensuring temporal and numeric consistency across the pipeline.
Whitespace, punctuation, and encoding normalisation to correct URL‑encoded strings, trailing spaces, apostrophes, hyphens, and other formatting artefacts identified during inspection.
Product‑name canonicalisation informed by inspection findings: truncated logistics names, SKU‑year mismatches, placeholder items, and token‑level similarity patterns. This stage prepares the ground for later fuzzy matching and reconciliation.
Category and department normalisation to harmonise behavioural and transactional taxonomies, which currently differ in granularity, casing, and punctuation.
Missing‑value handling for sparsely populated fields such as Order Zipcode, and for structurally empty fields like Product Description.
Grain‑appropriate restructuring to ensure that order‑item‑level logistics data and event‑level access logs can be aggregated, mapped, and compared consistently in downstream modelling.

The goal of this phase is not to perform modelling or feature engineering, but to produce a clean, canonical, and internally consistent Silver Layer that faithfully reflects the raw data while preserving the confirmed semantic distinctions (especially in geography) and resolving the structural issues surfaced during EDA. All transformations will be implemented transparently and sequentially, with explicit justification grounded in the inspection findings documented above.

title: Supply Chain and Customer Behaviour Analytics: End‑to‑End EDA, Data Quality Assessment, and Behavioural and Logistics Feature Engineering
text:
Silver Layer

Silver‑Layer Cleaning Plan (Canonical, Analysis‑Ready Schema)
The Bronze layer establishes a faithful but cleaned representation of the raw datasets: column names are standardised, canonical mappings are applied, dates are parsed, identifiers are fixed to string dtype, and whitespace is normalised. The Silver layer now focuses on transforming this structurally consistent Bronze dataset into an analysis‑ready, modelling‑ready schema. The goal is to remove unusable fields, enforce correct data types, normalise categorical structures, and prepare product and category fields for reconciliation with the access‑log dataset.
The Silver layer does not alter the semantic meaning of fields, and it does not perform feature engineering. Instead, it produces a clean, minimal, safe, and canonical dataset that is ready for enrichment, joining, and modelling.

Objectives of the Silver Layer

Remove columns that are structurally empty, masked, or unusable.
Remove or anonymise direct PII while preserving analytical geography.
Enforce numeric dtypes for financial, quantity, and ratio fields.
Validate and normalise categorical fields (department, category, segment).
Canonicalise product names and prepare them for fuzzy matching.
Ensure all identifiers are stable, string‑typed, and non‑numeric.
Validate geographic fields and preserve the store‑vs‑destination distinction.
Produce a minimal, analysis‑ready schema with no redundant or unusable fields.



Columns to Drop in the Silver Layer
These fields are either 100% empty, masked, or contain direct PII that cannot support analysis:

product_description — structurally empty in the raw export.
customer_email — masked; no analytical value.
customer_password — masked; no analytical value.
customer_first_name — direct PII; not needed for modelling.
customer_last_name — direct PII; not needed for modelling.
store_street — overly granular PII; city/state/country are sufficient.

These columns remain in Bronze for auditability but are removed in Silver.


Numeric Type Enforcement
The following fields should be converted to numeric types with errors='coerce':

product_price
order_item_product_price
order_item_quantity
order_item_discount
order_item_discount_rate
line_total_amount
sales
net_sales_amount
benefit_per_order
order_profit_per_order
shipping_days_actual
shipping_days_scheduled
order_item_profit_ratio

This ensures that financial and quantity fields behave correctly in aggregation, modelling, and statistical analysis.


Categorical Normalisation
The following fields should be converted to categorical dtype after trimming and deduplication:

department_name
category_name
customer_segment
shipping_mode
order_status
destination_market
destination_region
destination_state
store_state
store_country
destination_country

This reduces memory footprint and ensures consistent grouping behaviour.


Product‑Name Canonicalisation
Product names require structured normalisation before reconciliation with access logs:

lowercase normalisation
punctuation removal
whitespace compression
tokenisation for fuzzy matching
optional SKU‑year extraction
optional brand extraction

This step prepares the Silver layer for the product‑matching and enrichment logic that follows.


Geographic Validation
The Silver layer preserves the confirmed semantic distinction:

Store location (point of sale):
store_city, store_state, store_country, store_zipcode, store_latitude, store_longitude
Destination location (delivery region):
destination_city, destination_state, destination_country, destination_zipcode, destination_region, destination_market

These fields should be validated for consistency, missingness, and geographic plausibility.


Deduplication and Final Schema Validation
The Silver layer concludes with:

row‑level deduplication

column‑level validation

dtype summary

missingness summary

schema export for downstream modelling

The resulting dataset becomes the canonical input for the Gold layer (feature engineering, modelling, and enrichment).

title: Supply Chain and Customer Behaviour Analytics: End‑to‑End EDA, Data Quality Assessment, and Behavioural and Logistics Feature Engineering
section: Temporal Alignment Considerations Before Cross‑Dataset Matching
text:
Temporal Alignment Considerations Before Cross‑Dataset Matching
Before attempting any cross‑dataset product matching or behavioural‑to‑transactional modelling, it is essential to confirm whether the logistics and access‑log datasets operate within the same time window. Even though the Silver layer is now structurally clean and semantically consistent, the two datasets originate from different systems and may cover entirely different periods. If their temporal ranges do not overlap, then no customer in the access logs could have purchased the products recorded in the logistics dataset, and no browsing behaviour could be linked to any order.
This is a foundational requirement: cross‑dataset reconciliation is only meaningful when the datasets share a common temporal horizon.

Why Temporal Alignment Matters

Product matching becomes ambiguous if the access logs represent a different product catalogue period than the logistics dataset.
Behavioural modelling becomes invalid if browsing events occur months or years before or after the recorded purchases.
SKU‑level reconciliation may fail not because of naming inconsistencies, but because the products simply did not coexist in the same timeframe.
Customer‑level or session‑to‑order linkage becomes impossible without overlapping dates.

Given that the EDA revealed structural differences in product naming, SKU versions, and category granularity, it is especially important to rule out the possibility that these differences are driven by time‑period mismatches rather than naming inconsistencies alone.


What Needs to Be Verified
To justify any cross‑dataset integration, we must confirm:

the earliest and latest dates in the logistics dataset (order_date and shipping_date),
the earliest and latest timestamps in the access logs (date),
whether the two ranges overlap,
and if so, the size of the overlapping window.

If the datasets do not overlap in time, then:

product matching should not be attempted,
behavioural‑to‑transactional modelling is not meaningful,
and the two datasets should be analysed independently.

If they do overlap, then the next step is to quantify the overlap and proceed with product reconciliation and behavioural enrichment.


Next Step
Before moving forward with product matching or modelling, we should compute the temporal ranges of both datasets and verify whether they intersect. This will determine whether cross‑dataset integration is logically valid or whether the datasets should be treated as separate analytical domains.


=== LOGISTICS DATASET — TEMPORAL RANGE ===
Earliest order_date : 2015-01-01 00:00:00
Latest order_date   : 2018-01-31 23:38:00

=== ACCESS LOGS DATASET — TEMPORAL RANGE ===
Earliest access date : 2017-09-01 06:00:00
Latest access date   : 2018-01-31 23:58:00

=== TEMPORAL OVERLAP CHECK ===
Datasets DO overlap in time.
Overlap window: 2017-09-01 06:00:00 → 2018-01-31 23:38:00




Interpretation of Temporal Alignment Between Logistics and Access‑Log Datasets
The temporal‑range analysis confirms that the two datasets do not cover identical time periods, but they do share a meaningful overlap window. This has direct implications for whether cross‑dataset reconciliation, behavioural modelling, and product‑level integration are logically valid.

Logistics Dataset Time Window

Earliest order date: 2015‑01‑01
Latest order date: 2018‑01‑31

This indicates that the logistics dataset spans a multi‑year period (2015–2018), capturing historical order activity, product sales, and shipping performance across several years.


Access‑Log Dataset Time Window

Earliest access date: 2017‑09‑01
Latest access date: 2018‑01‑31

The access logs cover a much shorter period, beginning in late 2017 and ending at the same time as the logistics dataset. This dataset represents a narrow behavioural window focused on browsing activity, product views, and session‑level interactions.


Overlap Window

2017‑09‑01 → 2018‑01‑31

This is the period during which both datasets were active simultaneously. Only within this window can browsing behaviour and transactional outcomes be meaningfully related.


Implications for Cross‑Dataset Matching

Cross‑dataset product matching is logically valid, but only for products that appear in both datasets during the overlap window.
Behavioural‑to‑transactional modelling is valid only within the overlap period. Any attempt to link browsing behaviour to purchases outside this window would be invalid.
The majority of the logistics dataset (2015–2017) has no corresponding behavioural data, meaning:

those orders cannot be linked to access‑log sessions,
those products may not appear in the access logs,
and those customers cannot be matched to browsing behaviour.




Implications for Product‑Name Reconciliation
The earlier EDA revealed structural differences in product naming (truncation, punctuation, SKU‑year differences). The temporal analysis adds an important dimension:

Some mismatches may be due to products that existed in earlier years (2015–2016) but were no longer browsed in 2017–2018.
Conversely, some products in the access logs may represent newer catalogue items introduced after 2017.
Therefore, product matching should be performed with awareness that only products active during the overlap window are expected to match.



Implications for Modelling and Feature Engineering

Customer‑level or session‑to‑order linkage is only meaningful within the overlap window.
Behavioural features (session counts, product views, recency/frequency metrics) should be computed only for the overlapping period.
Order‑level modelling (profitability, shipping delays, discount behaviour) can still use the full logistics dataset, but behavioural enrichment must be restricted to the overlap.



Summary
The datasets do overlap in time, but only partially. This means:

Cross‑dataset matching is possible, but only for the 2017‑09 to 2018‑01 window.
Behavioural‑to‑transactional modelling is valid only within this window.
Product reconciliation must account for catalogue changes across years.
The majority of the logistics dataset remains valuable for operational analysis, but not for behavioural linkage.

This temporal insight ensures that the next steps—product matching, category harmonisation, and behavioural sessionisation—are grounded in a logically valid and temporally consistent foundation.

title: Supply Chain and Customer Behaviour Analytics: End‑to‑End EDA, Data Quality Assessment, and Behavioural and Logistics Feature Engineering
text:
Gold Layer

Gold‑Layer Plan (With Explicit Temporal‑Overlap Constraints)
The Gold layer builds on the structurally stable, semantically consistent Silver datasets to create enriched, modelling‑ready features. Given that the logistics and access‑log datasets only overlap between 2017‑09‑01 and 2018‑01‑31, all cross‑dataset integration must explicitly respect this temporal constraint. The Gold layer will therefore be split into two parallel tracks:

Within‑overlap, cross‑dataset enrichment and modelling

Full‑horizon, single‑dataset operational and descriptive modelling

This ensures that behavioural‑to‑transactional analysis is logically valid, while still extracting maximum value from the longer logistics history.


1. Temporal Filtering and Overlap Subsets
Objective: Create explicit overlap‑restricted subsets for any cross‑dataset work.

Define:

logistics_overlap: subset of fact_logistics_silver where order_date ∈ [2017‑09‑01, 2018‑01‑31].
access_overlap: subset of fact_access_logs_silver where date ∈ [2017‑09‑01, 2018‑01‑31].

All cross‑dataset joins, product matching, and behavioural‑to‑transactional modelling will operate only on these overlap subsets.
The full fact_logistics_silver remains available for long‑horizon operational and financial analysis.




2. Product‑Level Reconciliation (Overlap‑Restricted)
Objective: Reconcile product identities between logistics and access logs within the overlap window.

Use product_name_canonical (logistics) and canonicalised product (access logs).
Apply:

token‑based similarity,
fuzzy string matching (e.g., RapidFuzz),
optional brand and SKU‑year extraction.

Restrict matching to:

products that appear in logistics_overlap,
products that appear in access_overlap.

Output:

a product mapping table (logistics SKU ↔︎ access‑log product) valid only for the overlap period.

This mapping becomes the backbone for session‑to‑order and behavioural‑to‑transactional linkage.




3. Behavioural Sessionisation and Feature Engineering (Access Overlap)
Objective: Build session‑level and visitor‑level behavioural features within the overlap window.

On access_overlap:

define sessions (IP + inactivity threshold, e.g., 30 minutes),
compute:

session length (events, duration),
product views per session,
category/department diversity,
entry/exit pages,
simple bounce flags (single‑event sessions),
recency/frequency metrics per IP.


Use the product mapping table to:

tag sessions with matched logistics products where possible,
identify sessions that interacted with products that later appear in orders.





4. Order‑Level Enrichment Within the Overlap
Objective: Enrich orders in logistics_overlap with behavioural context where logically valid.

For each order in logistics_overlap:

link to matched products via the product mapping table,
aggregate behavioural features at:

product level (views before purchase),
IP or pseudo‑visitor level (if a safe linkage key exists),
temporal proximity (e.g., sessions in the X days before order_date).


Create enriched order‑level features such as:

pre‑purchase view counts,
category exploration depth,
time between last view and purchase (where linkable),
simple “seen before purchase” flags.


All such features are only computed for the overlap period and clearly documented as such.



5. Modelling Tracks

5.1 Behavioural‑to‑Transactional Modelling (Overlap‑Only)
Objective: Model how browsing behaviour relates to purchase outcomes, using only the overlap window.

Targets:

late delivery risk,
order profitability,
cancellation/return proxies (if derivable),
product‑level conversion proxies (where feasible).

Features:

enriched behavioural features (session and product level),
logistics features (shipping mode, region, segment, etc.).

Scope:

restricted to logistics_overlap with available behavioural enrichment.




5.2 Operational and Financial Modelling (Full Logistics Horizon)
Objective: Use the full fact_logistics_silver (2015–2018) for operational and financial modelling that does not depend on access logs.

Targets:

shipping delays,
late delivery risk,
profitability,
discount effectiveness,
regional performance.

Features:

logistics attributes only (no behavioural features).

This track leverages the longer time horizon for more robust temporal patterns and seasonality.





6. Clear Lineage and Documentation
Objective: Make temporal constraints explicit in all Gold‑layer assets.

Every Gold‑layer table and model will:

state whether it is **_overlap** only or **_gold** for full horizon,
document the exact date filters applied,
document whether behavioural features are present.

Example naming:

orders_gold_overlap_enriched (logistics + behaviour, 2017‑09 to 2018‑01),
orders_gold_full_operational (logistics only, 2015‑2018),
sessions_gold_overlap (session‑level features, 2017‑09 to 2018‑01).





7. Guardrails for Interpretation

Any insight that relies on behavioural features must be explicitly framed as valid only for the overlap period.
Any long‑term trend analysis (2015–2018) must be based solely on logistics data.
Product‑level conclusions must distinguish between:

products active only in the historical period,
products active during the overlap,
products appearing only in access logs.


This Gold‑layer plan ensures that all cross‑dataset integration is temporally coherent, analytically defensible, and clearly separated from broader operational modelling that uses the full logistics history.


=== LOGISTICS OVERLAP SHAPE === (13746, 48)
=== ACCESS OVERLAP SHAPE    === (466728, 11)





Logistics Dataset Feature Engineering
We now construct our logistics‑level feature table, which forms the foundation of the Gold Layer. This table enriches each order‑item record with revenue, net‑sales, shipping, profitability, temporal, and segmentation attributes. Below we outline the logic used to engineer these features.

How we compute logistics features
We operate at the order‑item grain, enhancing each row with derived financial, operational, and temporal metrics.


1. Revenue and net‑sales calculations

Revenue is computed either from line_total_amount (if available) or as
order_item_product_price × order_item_quantity.

Net sales amount subtracts the applied discount from revenue and is clipped at zero.

Net‑sales ratio expresses the proportion of revenue retained after discounting.

These fields establish the financial backbone for downstream customer and product aggregation.


2. Shipping duration and delay metrics

Actual shipping days are computed as the difference between shipping_date and order_date.

Shipping delay measures the deviation from the scheduled duration.

Delay flags classify whether an order was delayed (is_delayed).

Delay buckets segment orders into interpretable categories:
Early, On‑time, Slight Delay, Moderate Delay, Severe Delay.

These features capture operational reliability and fulfilment performance.


3. Profitability metrics

Margin ratio is computed as
benefit_per_order ÷ revenue,
providing a normalised profitability measure at the order‑item level.



4. Customer and product enrichment

High‑value order flag identifies orders whose net sales exceed twice the median order value.

Price buckets segment products into quantile‑based price tiers
(Very Low → Very High).

These enrichments support segmentation, ranking, and modelling tasks across domains.


5. Temporal features
From the order_date timestamp, we derive: - Order year
- Order month
- Day of week
- Order hour
These temporal attributes support seasonality analysis, demand modelling, and behavioural alignment with clickstream sessions.

Together, these engineered fields form the Gold‑Layer logistics table, providing a clean, enriched, and modelling‑ready foundation for customer‑level and product‑level feature construction.

Access Logs Feature Engineering
We now construct our access‑log Gold Layer, transforming raw web‑session activity into a structured, analytics‑ready behavioural dataset. This layer captures temporal patterns, product‑interaction signals, URL‑derived semantics, and session‑level engagement indicators. Below we outline the logic used to engineer these features.

How we compute access‑log features
We operate at the page‑view grain, enriching each row with derived temporal, behavioural, and URL‑based attributes before rolling up key indicators at the session level.


1. Canonical timestamp
We standardise the raw timestamp by converting session_date_time into a canonical datetime type, ensuring consistent temporal operations across the dataset.


2. Temporal features
From the canonical timestamp, we derive: - Session year
- Session month
- Day of week
- ISO week number
- Quarter
These attributes support seasonality analysis, traffic profiling, and alignment with logistics‑side order timelines.


3. Product‑view detection
A page view is classified as a product view if its URL contains /product/.
We encode this as a binary product_view_count feature at the row level.


4. Action‑based features derived from URL patterns
Since the Silver Layer does not include an explicit action column, we infer actions directly from URL structure: - Add‑to‑cart events (/add_to_cart)
- Product page views (/product/)
- Category page views (/category/)
- Department page views (/department/)
These binary indicators allow us to reconstruct behavioural funnels and engagement patterns.


5. URL‑derived semantic features
We extract structural information from the URL: - URL depth (number of / separators)
- Last URL token, capturing the terminal semantic element (e.g., product slug, category name)
These features help differentiate between shallow navigational pages and deep product‑level interactions.


6. Canonical product naming
We retain the canonical product name when available.
If missing, we generate a canonicalised version by lowercasing, stripping punctuation, and normalising whitespace.


7. Session‑level engagement flags and aggregates
Using session_id, we compute: - has_product_view — whether the session contains at least one product view
- has_add_to_cart — whether the session contains an add‑to‑cart event
- has_key_event — alias for add‑to‑cart, used for funnel analysis
- session_page_views — total page views in the session
- session_product_views — total product views in the session
These features provide a compact behavioural summary for each session, enabling downstream modelling and cross‑domain alignment with logistics‑side customer activity.


8. Final cleanup
We remove duplicate rows and reset the index to ensure a clean, consistent Gold‑Layer dataset.

Together, these engineered features form the Gold‑Layer access‑logs table, providing a structured, high‑resolution view of customer browsing behaviour, product interaction intensity, and session‑level engagement patterns.

Session Features
We now construct our session‑level feature table, which aggregates page‑view activity into meaningful behavioural summaries aligned with modern analytics standards. This Gold‑Layer table captures engagement, bounce behaviour, duration, temporal patterns, and IP‑level signals. Below we outline the logic used to engineer these features.

How we compute session features
We begin from the enriched access‑log Gold Layer and roll up activity at the session_id level, producing a compact representation of each browsing session.



1. Key‑event detection (GA4‑aligned)
We identify key events using the is_add_to_cart flag created in the access‑log Gold Layer.
If this field is missing, we fall back to URL‑based detection (/add_to_cart).
This aligns with GA4’s emphasis on meaningful interactions rather than simple page counts.



2. Date extraction
We derive a session_date field from the timestamp to support daily aggregation and temporal alignment with logistics‑side order activity.



3. Session‑level aggregation
Using session_id, we compute:

Session start and session end timestamps

Total page views

Unique URL actions (unique page URLs visited)

Key‑event count

Total product views

IP address associated with the session

This forms the core behavioural summary for each session.



4. Duration metrics
We compute:

duration_seconds — time between first and last event

duration_minutes — normalised duration

These metrics support engagement scoring and GA4‑style session classification.



5. Universal Analytics (UA) bounce definition
For historical comparison, we compute the legacy UA bounce flag:

A UA bounce occurs when a session has exactly one page view.

This definition is simple but limited, as it cannot distinguish between quick exits and long single‑page reads.



6. GA4 engagement definition
We adopt the modern GA4 definition of an engaged session, which is true if any of the following hold:

Session lasts more than 10 seconds, or

Session has 2 or more page views, or

Session contains at least one key event

This produces a more meaningful measure of user engagement.



7. GA4 bounce definition
GA4 bounce is defined as the inverse of engagement:

bounce_session_ga4 = not is_engaged_session_ga4

This aligns our behavioural modelling with current industry standards.



8. Duration buckets
We segment sessions into interpretable duration categories:

Bounce (0–1 min)

Short (1–5 min)

Medium (5–15 min)

Long (15–60 min)

Very Long (60+ min)

These buckets support funnel analysis, segmentation, and behavioural clustering.



9. Engagement score (custom metric)
We compute a composite engagement score using a weighted blend of:

Page views

Unique URL actions

Key events

This produces a continuous measure of session intensity beyond binary engagement flags.



10. Temporal features
From the session start timestamp, we derive:

Session hour

Day of week

These features support hourly traffic analysis, weekday/weekend patterns, and behavioural seasonality.



11. IP frequency encoding
We compute how frequently each IP appears across all sessions.
This helps identify:

heavy repeat visitors

shared devices

bots or automated traffic patterns


Together, these engineered features form the Gold‑Layer session table, providing a structured, GA4‑aligned, modelling‑ready view of user engagement, browsing behaviour, and session‑level interaction patterns.


Key Event Detection in Access Logs and Its Role in GA4 Engagement

Understanding Key Events in this Dataset
Although this dataset predates Google Analytics 4 (GA4), the raw access logs contain implicit user actions embedded directly in the URL path. These actions appear as suffixes appended to product URLs, such as:

/add_to_cart
(potentially other actions in the full dataset)

Example from the raw and silver layers:









sample_url
action




0
/department/fitness/category/baseball%20&%20softball/product/adidas%20Brazuca%202017%20Official%20Match%20Ball
no_action


1
/department/fitness/category/basketball/product/Diamondback%20Boys'%20Insight%2024%20Performance%20Hybr/add_to_cart

This pattern indicates that the user not only viewed a product page but also performed a meaningful interaction (e.g., adding an item to the cart). These interactions are the functional equivalent of GA4 key events.


How Key Events Are Detected
A key event is identified by checking whether the URL contains an action suffix. In this dataset, the primary action observed is:

add_to_cart

Key event detection is implemented as:
'fact["is_key_event"] = fact["page_url"].str.contains("add_to_cart", case=False, regex=False)'

Customer Features
Next, we compute our customer‑level feature table, forming a key component of the Gold Layer. Below we outline the logic used to derive these features from the cleaned and canonicalised logistics dataset.

How we compute customer features
We aggregate the dataset at the customer_id level to generate behavioural, financial, and lifecycle attributes. Specifically, we compute:


1. Core behavioural and financial metrics

Number of orders placed by each customer (unique order_id count).

Total revenue generated (net_sales_amount summed across orders).

Average revenue per order (net_sales_amount mean).

Average shipping duration (shipping_days_actual mean).

Late‑delivery rate (mean of the binary is_delayed flag).

Average margin ratio across purchased items.

Average discount applied to the customer’s orders.



2. Customer lifecycle metrics

First order date and last order date to establish the customer’s activity window.

Customer lifetime (days) calculated as the difference between first and last order.

Recency (days) measured as the number of days since the customer’s most recent order relative to the dataset’s maximum order date.



3. Order‑frequency segmentation
To support downstream modelling and segmentation, we bucket customers into interpretable frequency groups based on their total number of orders.
We use safe, strictly increasing bin edges to avoid monotonic‑binning errors:

1 order

2–3 orders

4–5 orders

6–10 orders

10+ orders

These engineered features form the Gold‑Layer customer table, produced for both the full logistics dataset and the overlap‑aligned subset used for cross‑domain modelling.
Together, they provide a consolidated, modelling‑ready view of customer behaviour, value, lifecycle, and operational experience.

Product Features
We now compute our product‑level feature table, forming another core component of the Gold Layer. This table captures demand patterns, pricing behaviour, operational performance, and lifecycle characteristics for each product. Below we outline the logic used to derive these features.

How we compute product features
We aggregate the logistics dataset at the product_name_canonical level, producing a consolidated view of product performance across orders, customers, and time. Specifically, we compute:


1. Demand and sales metrics

Total number of orders containing the product (unique order_id count).

Total units sold across all orders.

Average units per order (order_item_quantity mean).

Total revenue generated by the product (net_sales_amount sum).

Average revenue per order (net_sales_amount mean).

Average selling price (order_item_product_price mean).

Average discount applied to the product.

Average margin ratio across all sales.

High‑value order rate, capturing how often the product appears in high‑value baskets.



2. Operational performance metrics

Average shipping duration (shipping_days_actual mean).

Late‑delivery rate (mean of is_delayed).

Average shipping delay (shipping_delay mean).

These metrics help us understand not only how well a product sells, but also how reliably it is delivered.


3. Product lifecycle metrics

First sold date and last sold date, establishing the product’s active selling window.

Product lifetime (days) computed as the difference between first and last sale.

Recency (days) measured as the number of days since the product was last sold relative to the dataset’s maximum sale date.



4. Segmentation buckets
To support downstream modelling, ranking, and assortment analysis, we create interpretable product‑level segments:

Demand buckets based on quantiles of total orders
(Very Low → Very High).

Margin buckets based on quantiles of average margin ratio
(Very Low → Very High).

These engineered features form the Gold‑Layer product table, produced for both the full logistics dataset and the overlap‑aligned subset used for cross‑domain modelling.
Together, they provide a comprehensive, modelling‑ready view of product demand, profitability, operational behaviour, and lifecycle dynamics.

Gold Layer Outputs
The Gold layer now contains fully enriched, analysis‑ready datasets derived from the Silver layer. These represent the final, modelling‑ready assets for transactional, behavioural, customer, and product intelligence. Overlap tables ensure both behavioural and transactional datasets operate on a shared product universe.


1. fact_logistics_gold
Order‑level fact table containing: - Revenue, net sales, discount, and margin features
- Shipping durations, delays, delay buckets, delivery risk
- Product, customer, and store enrichment
- Temporal features (year, month, day‑of‑week, hour)
- Price buckets and high‑value order flags
- Cleaned categorical dimensions (category, department, shipping mode, destination)
This is the primary transactional fact table.



2. fact_logistics_gold_overlap
Filtered subset of fact_logistics_gold containing only: - Orders whose product universe overlaps with the access‑logs product universe
- Ensures behavioural → transactional alignment
- Supports cross‑dataset modelling (e.g., session → purchase propensity)
- Used for attribution, funnel analysis, and unified modelling
This is the transactional counterpart to sessions_features_gold_overlap.



3. fact_access_logs_gold
Page‑level behavioural fact table containing: - Product views, add‑to‑cart events, and URL‑derived features
- Session‑level flags (has_product_view, has_add_to_cart, has_key_event)
- Temporal features (year, month, week, quarter, day‑of‑week, hour)
- URL depth, page type, last token
- Cleaned canonical product names
- Session identifiers for grouping and funnel analysis
This is the raw behavioural foundation for session‑level aggregation.



4. fact_access_logs_gold_overlap
Filtered subset of fact_access_logs_gold containing only: - Behavioural events tied to products present in the logistics dataset
- Ensures both datasets share the same product domain
- Supports cross‑dataset modelling and behavioural attribution
This is the behavioural counterpart to fact_logistics_gold_overlap.



5. sessions_features_gold
Session‑level behavioural table containing: - Pageviews, unique URL actions, key event counts
- UA bounce definition (historical)
- GA4 engagement definition (modern)
- GA4 bounce definition
- Duration metrics and duration buckets
- Engagement score
- Temporal session features (hour, day‑of‑week)
- IP frequency encoding
- Session‑level behavioural summaries
This table captures user engagement and behavioural patterns.



6. sessions_features_gold_overlap
Filtered subset of sessions_features_gold containing only: - Sessions that interacted with products present in the logistics dataset
- Ensures behavioural data aligns with the transactional product universe
- Supports cross‑dataset modelling (e.g., session → purchase conversion)
This is the session‑level counterpart to fact_logistics_gold_overlap.



7. customer_features_gold
Customer‑level aggregated table containing: - Order counts, total revenue, average revenue
- Average shipping days, late rate
- Average margin and discount
- First/last order dates
- Customer lifetime and recency
- Order frequency buckets (robust to small datasets)
This is the customer dimension for segmentation, lifecycle analysis, and CLV‑style modelling.



8. customer_features_gold_overlap
Filtered subset of customer_features_gold containing only: - Customers whose orders appear in the product‑overlap universe
- Ensures customer‑level modelling aligns with behavioural + transactional overlap
- Useful for unified customer journey modelling



9. product_features_gold
Product‑level aggregated table containing: - Total orders and units sold
- Average units per order
- Total and average revenue
- Average price, discount, and margin
- High‑value order rate
- Shipping performance (avg delay, late rate)
- First/last sold dates
- Product lifetime and recency
- Demand and margin buckets
- Category and department
This is the product dimension for merchandising, pricing, and recommendation analytics.



Supporting Diagnostic Tables (EDA Utilities)
These tables support your EDA and validation workflows:

action_rows, non_action_rows, action_samples, non_action_sample — validate URL parsing and add‑to‑cart detection.

url_samples — validate URL tokenisation and page‑type extraction.

missing_df — missingness summary across all datasets.

access_overlap, logistics_overlap — product‑level overlap diagnostics.

Bronze/Silver tables — retained for lineage, debugging, and reproducibility.




Summary
Together, these Gold‑layer tables form a complete analytics foundation:

- modelling

Exploratory Summary: Key Patterns, Data Quality Signals, and Business Implications
This consolidated summary integrates statistical findings across all six Gold‑layer datasets and their overlap counterparts, reflecting the full operational, behavioural, customer, and product landscape. The patterns below highlight structural consistency, distributional behaviour, data quality signals, and business‑relevant insights that set the foundation for visual EDA and modelling.


Logistics: Operational Performance, Financial Structure, and Global Footprint

Shipping Performance and Delay Dynamics
The logistics dataset (fact_logistics_gold) shows highly consistent fulfilment behaviour: - Actual shipping times cluster between 2–5 days (mean ~3.47), with low skew and negative kurtosis. - Scheduled shipping averages 2.93 days, consistently underestimating actual fulfilment. - ~55% of orders are late, driven by: - tight SLAs, - systemic operational delays, - or business rules that classify minor overruns as “late”.
Delay buckets reinforce this: - Slight Delay dominates (84k orders), - Moderate Delay exists but is small, - Severe Delay is structurally absent (0 cases), - Early and On‑time are materially lower.
The overlap dataset (fact_logistics_gold_overlap) mirrors these patterns with similar means and distributions, confirming behaviourally‑linked orders follow the same operational rhythm.


Revenue, Discounts, and Profitability
Financial variables exhibit heavy right‑skew and extreme kurtosis: - net_sales_amount ranges from $4.99 → $1,879, with kurtosis > 24. - Discounts reach $500, with discount rates up to 25%. - Profit ratios include negative values, indicating: - loss‑leading promotions, - returns/refunds, - or cost attribution inconsistencies.
Overlap orders show higher average revenue (~$213 vs. $162), confirming that products with browsing activity tend to be higher‑value items.


Product and Category Structure

118 products, 50 categories, 11 departments in the full dataset.
Overlap dataset reduces to 71 products and 44 categories, dominated by:

Video Games,
Cleats,
Apparel,
Cameras,
Children’s Clothing.


This confirms that only a subset of products receive meaningful browsing activity, enabling funnel analysis.


Geographic and Market Distribution

Stores span 563 cities, 46 states, 2 countries (US + Puerto Rico).
Destinations span 164 countries and 23 regions, with strong presence in:

Western Europe,
Central America,
Southeast Asia,
South America.


Overlap destinations shrink to 41 countries, reflecting the narrower behavioural universe.


Temporal Coverage

Full logistics: Jan 2015 → Jan 2018 (1,126 days).
Overlap logistics: Sep 2017 → Jan 2018 (152 days), perfectly aligned with access logs.





Access Logs: Behavioural Dynamics, Engagement Patterns, and Funnel Structure

Traffic Volume and Temporal Patterns
fact_access_logs_gold contains 466k+ page‑level events: - Traffic peaks in September 2017, then stabilises across Oct–Jan. - Hour‑level activity peaks between 15:00–20:00. - Session timestamps are clean, continuous, and span 152 days.
Overlap logs mirror the full dataset exactly, confirming behavioural consistency across the shared product universe.


Page‑Type and Funnel Structure

URL structure is clean and hierarchical: /department/category/product/....
Product views are always present (product_view_count = 1), confirming correct parsing.
Add‑to‑cart events occur in 28% of pageviews.
Key events (product view or add‑to‑cart) occur in 65% of interactions.



Category and Department Behaviour

Departments: Outdoors, Apparel, Footwear, Fan Shop, Fitness, Golf dominate.
Categories: Cleats, Shop by Sport, Featured Shops, Apparel, Electronics lead.

This aligns strongly with logistics product performance.


IP and Session Quality

3,340 unique IPs, all valid.
High IP frequency (mean ~222) suggests:

shared networks,
repeat visitors,
or automated browsing patterns.






Session Features: Engagement, Bounce, and Duration Patterns
sessions_features_gold summarises 196k+ sessions: - Median session: 2 pageviews, 1–2 actions, 0 key events. - Heavy‑tailed behaviour: max 30 pageviews, 12 key events. - Duration is long‑tailed (0 → 14,640 seconds), with many zero‑duration sessions.
Bounce and engagement: - UA bounce rate: ~49% (96k sessions). - GA4 bounce rate: ~35% (69k sessions). - GA4 engagement is higher due to key‑event logic.
Duration buckets: - Long and Very Long dominate, - Bounce bucket is small but meaningful, - ~98k sessions have missing duration buckets due to zero duration.
Temporal behaviour: - Sessions evenly distributed across weekdays, - Slight peaks on Saturday, Monday, Sunday.
Overlap sessions mirror the full dataset exactly.



Customer Features: Lifecycle, Value, and Behavioural Diversity
customer_features_gold contains 20,652 customers: - Orders: median 3, max 15. - Revenue: $5 → $8,349, heavy‑tailed. - Average revenue per order: ~$190. - Shipping experience: avg shipping days ~3.47, late rate ~0.55. - Lifecycle: - Lifetime: 0 → 1,003 days, - Recency: 0 → 1,125 days.
Order buckets: - 1‑order customers dominate (8,884), - Only 116 customers have 10+ orders.
Overlap customers (10,014): - Almost entirely 1‑order customers (9,903), - Reflecting the short temporal window (152 days).



Product Features: Demand, Pricing, Margin, and Lifecycle
product_features_gold contains 118 products: - Total orders: 8 → 20,359, extremely skewed. - Total revenue: $2.4k → $5.5M. - Average price: $9.99 → $1,999.99. - Margin ratios stable (~0.12). - Demand and margin buckets evenly distributed across 5 levels. - Product lifetime: 1 → 1,003 days.
Overlap products (71): - Much shorter lifetimes (1 → 75 days), - Higher average prices, - Higher shipping delays, - Higher late rates.
This reflects the shorter, behaviourally‑linked product lifecycle.



Cross‑Dataset Alignment and Analytical Opportunities
The Gold layer now provides a unified analytical foundation:

Temporal alignment: Sep 2017–Jan 2018 across sessions and overlap orders.
Product alignment: 71 products shared across behavioural and transactional datasets.
Customer alignment: overlap customers represent short‑term, high‑intent behaviour.
Operational alignment: shipping delays and late rates consistent across all tables.

This enables: - session → order attribution, - behavioural → transactional funnel modelling, - customer segmentation, - product lifecycle modelling, - late delivery risk prediction, - discount elasticity analysis.

The Gold‑layer summaries reveal strong structural patterns, heavy‑tailed behaviour, and clear operational and behavioural signals across logistics, sessions, customers, and products. The following visualisations are recommended to explore these patterns in depth. They are grouped by analytical purpose and aligned with the characteristics of the current Gold tables.


1. Distribution and Shape of Key Numerical Variables
The Gold tables show significant skewness, heavy tails, and multimodal behaviour across revenue, discounts, shipping delays, engagement metrics, customer value, and product performance. Visualising these distributions helps identify outliers, validate modelling assumptions, and guide transformations.

4. Temporal Patterns and Seasonality

6. Relationship and Correlation Visualisations

7. Outlier and Anomaly Detection

8. Cross‑Dataset Alignment Visualisations
