title: Supply Chain and Customer Behaviour Analytics: End‑to‑End EDA, Data Quality Assessment, and Behavioural and Logistics Feature Engineering
text:
Exploratory Visualisations

1. DISTRIBUTION & SHAPE OF KEY NUMERICAL VARIABLES

Revenue-related distributions

Revenue Concentration and the Case for Log Transformation









The distribution reflects a classic heavy‑tailed revenue pattern where most orders fall within a narrow low‑value range, while a long, slowly declining tail extends toward higher transaction amounts. This indicates that the majority of sales are small, but a minority of large orders meaningfully stretch the upper range and shape the overall distribution. The steep rise near zero and gradual decay across the x‑axis confirm strong right‑skewness, making the distribution incompatible with symmetric assumptions such as normality.
The presence of extreme values means that averages, variance, and scale‑sensitive models can be disproportionately influenced by a small number of high‑value transactions. This behaviour is typical in retail and e‑commerce settings, where high‑value orders are rare but impactful.
A log transformation is well‑justified in this context. Applying log(net_sales_amount + 1) compresses extreme values, reduces skewness, and produces a more stable, interpretable distribution. This transformation improves the performance of linear models, stabilises variance, and reduces the dominance of outliers, allowing downstream modelling to focus on meaningful structural relationships rather than being distorted by a handful of large orders.


Line‑Level Value Concentration and the Structural Case for Log Transformation









The distribution shows the same structural signature observed in net_sales_amount: a dense concentration of small values and a long, gradually thinning right tail. Most line totals fall between 0 and roughly 500, with a clear peak in the low‑hundreds. As values increase, frequencies decline sharply, and only a small minority of transactions extend toward the upper end of the range. This produces a strongly right‑skewed, heavy‑tailed pattern.
Because line totals represent individual items rather than full orders, the concentration near zero is even more pronounced than in net sales. Net sales aggregate multiple line totals, so its tail extends further, but both variables reflect the same underlying commercial behaviour: many low‑value lines and a small number of high‑value ones. The shared skewness confirms that the distributional shape is intrinsic to the business process rather than an artefact of data quality.
This shape has practical implications. A small set of high‑value lines disproportionately influences averages, variance, and any model sensitive to scale or outliers. The asymmetry also violates assumptions of normality and homoscedasticity that many statistical and machine‑learning models rely on.
A log transformation is well‑suited here. Applying log(line_total_amount + 1) compresses extreme values, reduces skewness, and produces a more stable, interpretable distribution. It also aligns the scale of line totals more closely with net sales, improving coherence across features and supporting more robust downstream modelling.


Revenue Concentration, Heavy‑Tail Dynamics, and the Case for Log Transformation









The distribution shows a dense cluster of low‑revenue orders followed by a long, gradually tapering right tail. This pattern indicates that most transactions generate relatively small revenue, while a much smaller subset contributes disproportionately large amounts. The heavy tail is economically meaningful: high‑value orders are rare but exert strong influence on the overall distribution, stretching the upper range and pulling the mean upward. As a result, the “typical” order is far smaller than what the average suggests.
This asymmetry has direct analytical implications. The dominance of low‑value orders combined with a minority of extreme values creates a distribution that violates assumptions of symmetry and constant variance. Models that rely on normality or homoscedasticity will struggle, and summary statistics such as the mean or standard deviation will be heavily influenced by outliers rather than representative behaviour.
A log transformation is well suited for this variable. Applying log(revenue + 1) compresses extreme values, reduces skewness, and produces a more balanced, stable distribution. This transformation improves interpretability, stabilises variance, and makes relationships with other features more linear and model‑friendly. It also ensures that downstream modelling focuses on structural patterns rather than being dominated by a small number of high‑value transactions.



Discounts & profit ratios

Extreme Discount Outliers and Their Operational and Modelling Implications
















The distribution shows a dense concentration of very small discounts, followed by a long stretch of unusually large values. The boxplot reinforces this pattern: the interquartile range is tightly compressed near zero, the upper whisker rises only modestly, and then a large number of individual points extend far beyond it. This structure signals the presence of extreme outliers—discounts that are far larger than what the underlying distribution would naturally produce.
Several explanations can account for these extremes. Some may stem from operational or data‑entry anomalies, such as manual overrides, bulk adjustments, or inconsistent application of discount rules. Others may reflect legitimate policy exceptions, including clearance events, special customer tiers, or product‑specific promotions. Mixed discount mechanisms can also create artificial outliers if percentage‑based and absolute‑value discounts are stored in the same field. In some cases, the highest values may indicate data quality issues such as mis‑scaled entries, duplicated discounts, or incorrect currency conversions.
From a modelling perspective, these outliers can distort averages, inflate variance, and weaken relationships with revenue, margin, or product attributes. A log transformation helps stabilise the distribution by compressing extreme values and reducing skewness, but direct inspection of the largest discounts is still advisable. Understanding whether they represent genuine business behaviour or require cleaning or capping will improve both interpretability and downstream model performance.
















The discount rate distribution is tightly concentrated in a narrow band, with most values clustered between roughly 0.05 and 0.15 and a clear peak around the midpoint of that range. The histogram shows a steep rise near the lower end, followed by a smooth decline, which signals a right‑skewed distribution but with far less extreme tail behaviour than the absolute discount amounts. The boxplot reinforces this: the interquartile range is compact, the median sits cleanly inside it, and the whiskers extend only modestly before reaching the maximum. There are no extreme spikes or long chains of outliers, which means discount rates are applied in a more controlled and consistent way.
This pattern suggests that a relatively small subset of transactions does not dominate the discount rate landscape. Instead, the business appears to operate with a fairly standardised discounting policy, where most items receive similar percentage reductions. The absence of extreme outliers implies that discount rates are likely governed by rules or automated logic rather than manual overrides. This contrasts with absolute discount amounts, where large outliers can arise from high‑priced items or operational inconsistencies.
From an analytical perspective, the stability of the discount rate distribution makes it a more reliable feature for modelling than raw discount amounts. It is less sensitive to product price variation and less prone to distortion from rare, high‑value transactions. The narrow range also means transformations are generally unnecessary, and the variable can be used directly in models without risking undue influence from a small number of extreme cases.
















Profitability patterns and risk signals The distribution shows that most orders cluster very close to zero profit, with a substantial portion dipping into negative territory. This creates a left‑skewed shape where losses extend into a long, heavy tail. That tail indicates that while small losses are common, a meaningful number of orders incur large negative margins, and these extreme cases stretch far beyond the typical range. The boxplot reinforces this pattern: the central box is narrow and positioned near zero, while a dense chain of outliers extends deep into the negative side, signalling frequent and sometimes severe unprofitable transactions.
Several risks emerge from this structure. A concentration of orders near break‑even suggests thin margins overall, leaving little buffer for operational variability. The heavy negative tail implies that certain products, customer segments, or fulfilment scenarios systematically generate losses, which can distort financial performance and mask underlying inefficiencies. These extreme negative values also inflate variance, making profitability harder to model and predict. They may reflect legitimate business strategy—such as loss‑leader items or aggressive discounting—but they can also point to data quality issues, pricing misalignment, or fulfilment cost overruns.
From an analytical standpoint, the combination of skewness and heavy tails makes raw profit values unstable for modelling. A transformation such as log‑scaling (applied to shifted values) or winsorisation can help stabilise variance and reduce the influence of extreme losses. More importantly, the tail warrants targeted investigation to determine whether the negative outliers represent intentional strategy, operational leakage, or data anomalies.



Shipping delays
















Interpretation:
Shipping duration patterns and operational implications The distribution of actual shipping days shows a clear centre of gravity around two days, with smaller but consistent peaks at three, four, five, and six days. This pattern indicates a typical delivery window of 2–4 days, with most orders falling into a predictable operational rhythm. The small peak at zero days likely reflects same‑day fulfilment or local pickup scenarios rather than true zero‑day shipping.
The delay distribution reinforces this structure. Distinct peaks at integer values—especially at 1, 0, and −2—suggest that delays tend to occur in discrete steps rather than as a continuous spread. A delay of one day appears to be the most common deviation, while negative delays (early deliveries) also occur frequently enough to form their own mode. The presence of both early and late deliveries indicates variability in fulfilment efficiency, carrier performance, or customer proximity.
Operationally, the combination of tight clustering and stepped delays points to a system that is generally consistent but subject to systematic rather than random deviations. Regular one‑day delays may reflect bottlenecks in packing, handoff to carriers, or regional processing capacity. Early deliveries may indicate overestimation in scheduled times or faster‑than‑expected carrier performance. The absence of a long right tail is encouraging: extreme delays are rare, which reduces customer dissatisfaction risk and stabilises service‑level metrics.
From an analytics perspective, the stepped nature of delays suggests that modelling should treat delay as an ordinal or discrete variable rather than assuming continuous behaviour. It also highlights the value of segmenting by region, carrier, and product type to identify where the one‑day delay mode is most pronounced. Understanding these patterns can help target operational improvements and refine delivery‑time predictions.


Session-level behaviour






























Interpretation:
Engagement patterns and session duration The distribution shows that most sessions are short, with page views, unique URL actions, and key events all clustering near the lower end of their respective scales. Each metric exhibits a steep drop‑off as counts increase, which signals a strong engagement skew: a large volume of low‑interaction sessions and a progressively smaller number of highly engaged ones. This is typical of web behaviour, where casual browsing dominates and only a minority of users explore deeply or trigger meaningful events.
The duration distribution reinforces this pattern. Even on a log scale, most sessions sit at the lower end, while a long right tail stretches toward much longer durations. These long‑lived sessions represent a small but important subset of users who spend significantly more time on the site. They often correspond to high‑intent behaviour—product comparison, research, or multi‑step journeys—and can be disproportionately valuable for conversion modelling.
Implications for modelling Heavy skew requires transformation. Raw counts and durations will produce unstable variance and can dominate model behaviour. Log or square‑root transformations help stabilise these metrics and make relationships more linear.
Long‑tail users should be treated as a distinct behavioural segment. Their patterns differ meaningfully from the majority, and models that assume a single homogeneous distribution may underperform.
Engagement metrics are not interchangeable. Page views, unique actions, and key events each decay at different rates, suggesting they capture different layers of user intent. Feature engineering should preserve these distinctions rather than collapsing them into a single score.
Duration is especially sensitive to outliers. Even after log transformation, the longest sessions may require capping or winsorisation to prevent undue influence on predictive models.


Product Prices











Interpretation of Price Range and Product Positioning
The distribution of product prices reveals a strategy heavily skewed toward the “entry-to-mid-level” market, with a long tail of premium offerings that define the catalog’s aspirational limit:

The Volume Core ($0–$250): The vast majority of the product catalog is concentrated under the $250 mark. This dense cluster represents high-velocity, “commodity” items where competition is likely based on volume and fulfillment speed. Within this range, there is a sub-peak around $50–$100, suggesting a “sweet spot” for impulsive or routine consumer purchases.
The Mid-Tier Gap ($250–$600): There is a noticeable thinning of product density as prices move toward the $500 mark. This suggests the business may have a “missing middle”—a lack of “prosumer” or mid-tier lifestyle products that bridge the gap between basic sports gear and professional-grade equipment.
The Premium Tier ($600–$2,000): The catalog features a sparse but consistent “long tail” extending up to nearly $2,000. These items are likely technical equipment (e.g., high-end bicycles, professional treadmills, or premium electronics). While they represent a small fraction of total unit volume, they are critical for maintaining the brand’s positioning as a serious sporting goods provider.
Strategic Positioning Assessment: - Market Role: The business is positioned as an accessible mass-market retailer.

Risk: With such a high concentration in the low-price bracket, the business is extremely sensitive to the margin erosion and shipping delays identified in previous analyses. A $10 margin hit on a $50 item is catastrophic (20% loss), whereas the same hit on a $1,000 item is negligible.
Opportunity: Diversifying the $300–$700 range could improve overall Average Order Value (AOV) without requiring the high-touch sales effort associated with the $1,500+ premium tier.

Pricing Granularity: The price points appear highly standardized (noted by the “spikes” in the distribution), suggesting a rigid pricing architecture rather than dynamic, market-responsive pricing. This rigidity might explain why aggressive discounting is often needed to move inventory that doesn’t hit these pre-set psychological price points.


Summary: The catalog is optimized for accessibility and high turnover. However, the extreme density at the low end of the price spectrum makes the business model vulnerable to operational inefficiencies, as there is very little “price buffer” to absorb the high rates of late delivery and heavy discounting observed elsewhere in the data.


Violin plot: revenue by department

















Revenue patterns across departments
The boxplot and violin plot together show that revenue is highly unevenly distributed across departments. Several departments (Book Shop, Pet Shop, Discs Shop) have tightly clustered revenue with low medians and narrow spreads, indicating low variance and predictable performance. Others—especially Technology, Footwear, Outdoors, and Golf—show wide revenue ranges, many outliers, and long right‑tails, signalling high‑variance revenue behaviour driven by a mix of low‑value and occasional high‑value transactions.
Key high‑variance areas include: - Technology — the widest spread, many extreme outliers, and a long tail of high‑value orders. - Footwear — broad distribution with multiple high‑revenue points. - Outdoors & Golf — moderate medians but large spreads, suggesting occasional high-ticket items. - Health & Beauty — mid‑range median but noticeable variability.
Low‑variance departments include: - Book Shop - Pet Shop - Discs Shop These show compact distributions with few outliers, indicating stable, low‑value revenue patterns.



Why the violin plot struggled to scale
The violin plot uses kernel density estimation (KDE) to show the full shape of the distribution. KDE assumes a smooth, continuous distribution and tries to stretch across the entire numeric range. Revenue, however, is:

Extremely right‑skewed
Heavy‑tailed
Full of extreme outliers
Highly uneven across categories

This causes the KDE to stretch dramatically to accommodate the largest values (e.g., Technology’s high‑revenue outliers). As a result:

The violins become thin and compressed for low‑variance departments.
The density collapses near zero for most categories.
The plot loses meaningful shape because the KDE is dominated by a few extreme values.
The visual becomes unbalanced, with one or two departments dictating the entire x‑axis scale.

In short, the violin plot cannot display the true distribution shape when the data is too skewed and too sparse at the high end.



Why the boxplot handles this variable better
The boxplot is robust to skewness and outliers because it summarizes the distribution using quartiles and whiskers rather than density estimation. This makes it ideal for revenue:

It clearly shows median differences across departments.
It highlights IQR width, making variance comparisons straightforward.
Outliers are shown as points without distorting the entire scale.
The shape is not dependent on KDE smoothing, so extreme values don’t collapse the plot.

For heavy‑tailed variables like revenue, the boxplot provides a clean, interpretable, and stable view of cross‑department differences.



Violin plot: shipping delay by region

















Regions with consistently higher delays
Several regions show delay distributions shifted toward positive values, meaning late deliveries occur more frequently and with greater spread. These regions combine both higher average delays and wider variability:

Central Africa, West Africa, East Africa — the widest violins, with density concentrated around +1 to +3 days and long spreads. This pattern reflects structural constraints such as customs processing, long-haul routing, and inconsistent carrier performance.
South America — a broad distribution with a noticeable right shift, indicating recurring late deliveries and operational unpredictability.
Central Asia and South Asia — moderate but consistent positive skew, suggesting systemic bottlenecks in cross-border logistics or long transit chains.

These regions represent the highest operational risk, where delays are both common and volatile.



Regions with stable or early deliveries
Some regions cluster tightly around zero or even show density in negative values (early deliveries):

Western Europe, Northern Europe, Eastern Europe — narrow, centered violins with minimal spread, indicating highly predictable delivery performance.
US Center, East of USA, West of USA — compact distributions with slight negative skew, reflecting efficient domestic logistics and reliable carrier networks.
Canada — moderate spread but still centered near zero, showing stable cross-border operations.

These regions exhibit low variance and high reliability, making them operationally low-risk.



Regions with the highest variability
Variability is visible where the violin widens across multiple delay values:

Central Africa, West Africa, East Africa — the broadest shapes, spanning early to multi-day delays.
South America and Central Asia — wide distributions with no dominant mode, indicating inconsistent transit performance.
Oceania — moderate delays but a long spread, reflecting long-distance shipping and carrier variability.

High variance complicates forecasting, SLA commitments, and customer communication.



Operational implications

SLA exposure is highest in regions with wide, right-skewed violins; these areas are more likely to breach delivery promises.
Carrier and route optimization is most impactful in Africa, South America, and Central Asia, where delays appear systemic rather than random.
Inventory positioning may need to shift toward regional hubs or forward-stocking to stabilize delivery times.
Customer ETA messaging should be dynamic in high-variance regions, while stable regions can support tighter delivery windows.

Understanding these patterns helps prioritize where operational improvements will yield the greatest reliability gains.



Violin plot: engagement by day of week










Engagement patterns across the week
The distribution of engagement scores shows that most days share a broadly similar pattern, with a dense concentration of low‑to‑moderate engagement and a long right tail representing a smaller group of highly engaged sessions. Even with this shared structure, the violins reveal meaningful differences in both central tendency and variability across days.

Tuesday and Wednesday show the widest violins, indicating a larger spread of engagement scores and a higher density of mid‑to‑high engagement sessions. These days appear to support deeper browsing or more intentional user activity.
Friday and Monday display moderate variability, with a noticeable presence of higher engagement values but a more compact core distribution. These days likely reflect mixed behaviour—routine weekday usage with occasional spikes.
Thursday and Saturday show narrower distributions, suggesting more consistent but generally lower engagement. These days may reflect lighter browsing patterns or shorter sessions.
Sunday has one of the tightest distributions, with fewer high‑engagement outliers, indicating more uniform and typically lower engagement behaviour.




Peak engagement days
The strongest engagement signals appear on:

Tuesday
Wednesday

Both days show: - Wider distributions - Higher density in the upper engagement range - More pronounced tails of high‑engagement sessions
This suggests that mid‑week activity is not only more frequent but also more intensive, with users more likely to explore, interact, or complete deeper journeys.



Interpretation and operational implications

Mid‑week optimization: Tuesday and Wednesday are prime candidates for launching content, campaigns, or feature rollouts that benefit from higher engagement.
Weekend behaviour: Saturday and Sunday show more uniform, lower engagement, which may reflect casual browsing rather than high‑intent activity.
User segmentation: The long right tails on high‑engagement days indicate a subset of power users whose behaviour may be worth modelling separately.
Predictive modelling: Day‑of‑week should be included as a categorical feature, as it captures meaningful behavioural variance and improves session‑level predictions.

Understanding these patterns helps align engagement strategies with natural user rhythms and supports more accurate behavioural modelling.




2. CATEGORICAL DISTRIBUTIONS & CONCENTRATION PATTERNS

Scale of late vs on‑time deliveries









The distribution shows a clear dominance of late deliveries relative to on‑time performance. Using the approximate counts:

Late delivery ≈ 95,000
Shipping on time ≈ 40,000
Advance shipping ≈ 45,000
Shipping canceled ≈ 10,000

Late deliveries alone exceed the on‑time category by a large margin. The ratio of late to on‑time deliveries is roughly:

Late vs On‑time ≈ 95,000 / 40,000 ≈ 2.4×

This means late deliveries occur more than twice as often as on‑time deliveries.
When comparing late deliveries to the combined volume of all “successful” outcomes (advance + on‑time):

Advance + On‑time ≈ 85,000
Late ≈ 95,000

Even together, early and on‑time shipments still fall short of the late‑delivery count.


Interpretation and operational implications

Late deliveries represent the single largest delivery outcome, indicating a systemic reliability issue rather than isolated exceptions.
The gap between late and on‑time deliveries suggests persistent bottlenecks in fulfillment, carrier performance, or regional logistics.
The high volume of advance shipments highlights variability rather than consistent performance, reinforcing the need for tighter process control.
SLA compliance, customer satisfaction, and forecasting accuracy are all likely impacted by this imbalance.

Understanding which regions, carriers, or product types contribute most to late deliveries would help target the most impactful operational improvements.



Transaction Types










Distribution of transaction types
The mix of transaction types shows a clear hierarchy in operational volume, with one category dominating and the others forming a secondary tier. The approximate counts are:

DEBIT ≈ 70,000 — the largest category by a wide margin, representing the core transactional load.
TRANSFER ≈ 50,000 — substantial volume, forming the second‑largest operational stream.
PAYMENT ≈ 42,000 — close to transfers, indicating steady and frequent usage.
CASH ≈ 20,000 — the smallest category, less than one‑third of debit volume.

This distribution indicates that the system is primarily driven by electronic, account‑based activity, with cash transactions forming a much smaller share.



Operational focus implied by the mix

Debit transactions dominate workload. With nearly 70k occurrences, debit operations likely drive the majority of system throughput, error handling, and performance considerations. Any optimization in this category yields the highest operational impact.
Transfers and payments form a strong mid‑tier. Their combined volume (~92k) rivals debit activity, suggesting that inter‑account movement and bill‑payment workflows are also central to user behaviour.
Cash transactions are niche. Their lower volume implies limited operational strain but may require specialized handling due to physical or compliance‑related processes.
Electronic-first behaviour. The dominance of debit, transfer, and payment categories indicates a user base that relies heavily on digital financial interactions rather than cash‑based workflows.




Strategic implications

System reliability and scaling should prioritize debit and transfer pipelines, as disruptions here would affect the majority of users.
Fraud monitoring may need differentiated rules: debit and transfer categories carry higher exposure due to volume and transaction value patterns.
User experience improvements should focus on debit and payment flows, where even small friction reductions can meaningfully improve overall satisfaction.
Cash workflows may be candidates for automation or simplification, given their lower frequency but potentially higher handling cost.

Understanding how these transaction types evolve over time—especially shifts between debit, transfer, and payment—can help anticipate operational load and guide resource allocation.



Distribution of Customer Segments
























Dominant customer segments
The customer mix is heavily concentrated in one segment:

Consumer — the largest segment by a wide margin, forming the core of the customer base.
Corporate — a meaningful secondary segment, but far smaller than Consumer.
Home Office — the smallest segment, representing a niche portion of demand.

This distribution indicates that the business is primarily B2C‑driven, with B2B activity present but not dominant.



Dominant departments
Across departments, a few categories clearly lead in volume:

Fan Shop — the highest‑volume department, suggesting strong demand for fan‑related merchandise.
Apparel — a major contributor, reflecting broad and consistent consumer interest.
Golf — another high‑volume department, indicating a strong sports‑oriented customer base.

Lower‑volume departments such as Book Shop, Health and Beauty, and Pet Shop play smaller, more specialized roles.
This mix shows a product ecosystem centered around sports, apparel, and fan merchandise, with niche categories supplementing the assortment.



Dominant product categories

This tells us that the massive revenue drop observed in the raw data cannot be explained by historical seasonality or the calculated trend. This indicates that deep discounting is often applied to lower-value inventory or fails to incentivize larger basket sizes.
Absence of a “Volume Engine”: In the product and customer domains, the correlation between avg_discount and total_orders is remarkably weak. This suggests that the discounting strategy is not functioning as a high-velocity acquisition tool; it is not systematically driving more frequent purchases or significantly higher unit turnover.
Customer Value Misalignment: The data reveals that high-value customers (those with high total revenue and lifetime days) actually show a lower correlation with avg_discount than lower-tier segments. This implies that the business may be “over-discounting” to price-sensitive, low-loyalty users, while the core revenue-driving segment is less motivated by these incentives.
Strategic Risk: With a significant portion of orders already flagged for late_delivery_risk, combining poor fulfillment with margin-eroding discounts creates a “double-negative” effect—where the business pays a premium (via lost margin) for a customer experience that is statistically likely to be sub-optimal.

Overall, the current discounting model acts as a margin drain rather than a growth lever. The business is sacrificing profitability without a proportional gain in market share or order frequency, suggesting a need to pivot toward value-based pricing or targeted loyalty rewards rather than broad-spectrum discounting.


Planned vs. Actual Shipping Times









The comparison between scheduled logistics and real-world performance reveals a pervasive operational lag, indicating that the fulfillment system consistently over-promises and under-delivers:

A Systematic Performance Gap: The distribution of shipping_days_actual is significantly shifted to the right compared to shipping_days_scheduled. While the scheduled mean is a lean 2.93 days, the actual mean stretches to 3.47 days. This persistent delta of approximately 0.5 days suggests that the scheduling logic fails to account for recurring bottlenecks in the fulfillment chain.
Over-Optimistic Scheduling: The scheduled shipping times are highly concentrated around 2 and 4 days, reflecting a rigid, standardized planning model. However, the actual shipping times show a broader, more volatile spread, with a notable volume of orders taking 5 to 6 days—territory that is almost never accounted for in the “Planned” phase.
High Frequency of “Slight Delays”: Statistics show that 54% of orders are categorized as “Late delivery.” When cross-referenced with the visual, most of these fall into the “Slight Delay” bucket. While these delays are numerically small, their high frequency (over 98,000 occurrences) points to a structural inability to hit the “Standard Class” targets that make up the majority of the shipping volume.
Reliability Decay: The “On-time” and “Early” categories are significantly smaller than the “Late” categories. This imbalance indicates that the current logistics baseline is not a “normal distribution” centered on the target, but rather a “skewed distribution” where the target represents a best-case scenario rather than an average expectation.
Operational Impact: With actual shipping days frequently hitting the 5-6 day mark while customers are likely quoted a 3-4 day window, the business faces a constant “trust deficit.” This systematic deviation likely contributes to the high late_delivery_risk identified in the domain correlation matrices, potentially acting as a silent churn driver even when individual delays seem manageable.

Overall, the logistics data highlights a “planning-execution” mismatch. The business is operating on an idealistic schedule that does not reflect its 3.47-day reality, suggesting that either the delivery promises need to be recalibrated or the fulfillment process requires a fundamental injection of speed to meet the current 3-day target.


Price–Margin Trade-offs and Product Positioning









The analysis of product pricing against margin ratios reveals a distinct strategic divide in the catalog, highlighting how different price points contribute to the firm’s bottom line:

The High-Volume “Stability” Zone: A massive concentration of products exists in the $50 to $250 price range, maintaining a consistent profit margin ratio between 0.1 and 0.4. This represents the “engine room” of the business—standardized inventory with predictable returns that sustain the operational baseline.
Extreme Margin Volatility in Low-Cost Items: For products priced under $100, the margin ratio is highly unpredictable, with significant clusters falling into negative territory (down to -2.75). This indicates that low-priced items are often used as “loss leaders” or are disproportionately affected by fixed costs and heavy discounting, leading to significant margin erosion.
The Premium Ceiling: As product prices scale toward the $1,500–$2,000 range, the margin ratio does not continue to climb. Instead, it stabilizes or slightly tightens around the 0.2 to 0.4 mark. This suggests that premium products carry higher absolute dollar profits but do not necessarily offer superior percentage margins compared to mid-tier goods, likely due to higher acquisition or fulfillment costs for luxury inventory.
Strategic Outliers (High-Margin Niche): There are rare instances of products maintaining maximum margin ratios (~0.5) at mid-to-high price points ($500+). These represent the “ideal” positioning—high-value items that have escaped the commodity pricing trap and maintain maximum profitability.
Risk of “Profit Black Holes”: The presence of deep negative outliers suggests that a segment of the product portfolio is actively destroying value. These items likely suffer from a combination of high returns, excessive logistics costs (linked to the 3.47-day actual shipping average), or aggressive liquidations.

Overall, the product positioning reflects a business heavily reliant on the $100–$500 sweet spot. While the catalog attempts to play in the premium space, the real financial risk lies in the low-cost segment, where inconsistent margins and “loss-leader” behavior threaten to offset the gains made by higher-performing categories.


Pairplots: Logistics Dataset










Multivariate Relationships (Financial vs. Operational Metrics)
The pair plot analysis provides a multi-dimensional view of how business volume, pricing, and logistics interact, revealing the core mechanics of the revenue engine:

Revenue-Sales Synergy: A near-perfect linear correlation exists between sales and revenue. This indicates a highly transparent transaction model where top-line growth is driven directly by volume and price without significant interference from hidden fees or secondary revenue streams.
The Price-Profit Disconnect: The relationship between product_price and benefit_per_order shows significant dispersion. While higher-priced products generally contribute to larger absolute benefits, the wide vertical spread suggests that profitability is highly sensitive to other variables—likely the order_item_discount and shipping_days_actual identified in previous analyses. High price does not automatically guarantee high net benefit.
Logistics as a Constant Friction: shipping_days_actual appears as a “vertical band” across most financial metrics. Whether an order is high-revenue or low-revenue, it remains subject to the same 3.47-day average delay. This lack of correlation suggests the business does not currently prioritize faster shipping for high-value orders, representing a missed opportunity for tiered service levels.
Volume Concentration: The density plots (diagonal) reveal that the vast majority of orders are concentrated at the lower end of the price and revenue spectrum. This confirms a business model reliant on high-frequency, low-to-mid-value transactions rather than a “low-volume, high-premium” strategy.
Operational Constraints on Scaling: There is no evidence in the multivariate view that increasing sales volume leads to better shipping performance (economies of scale). Instead, the logistics metrics remain stubbornly static regardless of the financial throughput, suggesting that the current fulfillment infrastructure is operating at a fixed capacity that cannot easily flex with demand spikes.

Overall, the multivariate landscape depicts a business with a very clean sales-to-revenue pipeline but a disjointed relationship between pricing and actual profit. The “flatness” of the logistics response across all price points identifies a critical area where operational rigidity may eventually throttle financial growth.



Pairplots: Sessions Dataset

Joint Distributions (Engagement-Related Metrics)









The joint distribution analysis (hexbin plots) provides a dense visualization of user behavior clusters, moving beyond simple averages to show where the “mass” of the user base actually sits:

Engagement vs. Page Views (High-Density Core): The relationship shows a very tight, linear concentration. The “hottest” part of the hexbin is located at approximately 8–10 page views and an engagement score of 7–8. This represents the standard “active session” profile for the platform. The lack of significant dispersion suggests that page views are the primary and most predictable component of the engagement score calculation.
Action Efficiency (Unique Actions vs. Score): Similar to page views, unique URL actions show a high-density primary cluster. However, the distribution begins to “fan out” slightly more at the higher ends (Scores > 15), indicating that “power users” engage in a more diverse set of actions that create variance in their total score compared to the average user.
The Activity Threshold: In both distributions, there is a clear “empty zone” at the very bottom-left. Users don’t just “linger”; they either bounce immediately (not shown in this high-intensity view) or they quickly move into the 5+ action / 5+ score bracket. This suggests that the platform’s interface is effective at funneling users into active participation once they pass the initial landing phase.
Frequency of “Hyper-Active” Outliers: The lighter hexbins extending toward the top-right (Scores of 25–35) show that while power users exist, they are statistically rare compared to the massive central cluster. These users are likely the same ones identified in the Key Event analysis as having the highest conversion rates, but they represent a very small fraction of total session volume.
Uniformity of Experience: The lack of multiple distinct “hot spots” (multimodal distribution) suggests that the user base interacts with the platform in a relatively singular, uniform way. There aren’t two different types of users (e.g., “fast browsers” vs. “slow researchers”); rather, there is one dominant behavior pattern that simply scales in intensity.

Overall, the joint distributions confirm that Engagement Score is a high-fidelity proxy for session volume. The business can rely on this single metric to segment its audience, as it perfectly captures the density of both page exploration and unique interactions.




7. OUTLIER & ANOMALY DETECTION

Boxplots for Extreme Values and Outliers































Anomalies (Extreme Discounts, Negative Margins, and Fraud Signals)
The analysis of outlier behavior reveals critical pockets of financial leakage and operational irregularities that deviate from the standard business model:

Extreme Discount Anomalies: The distribution of order_item_discount identifies a significant “tail” of transactions where discounts exceed $400. Statistical summaries show a max discount of $500, which in many cases exceeds the average product price. This represents a “deep discount” cluster that does not correlate with increased volume, suggesting either aggressive liquidation of stale inventory or potential systematic abuse of promotional codes.
The “Profit Black Hole” (Negative Margins): A concerning number of transactions exhibit a negative order_item_profit_ratio, with extreme outliers reaching as low as -2.75. These are not minor losses; they indicate cases where the cost of fulfillment and discounting is nearly triple the revenue generated. These “black holes” are most prevalent in low-cost categories where fixed shipping overheads (linked to the 3.47-day actual shipping average) likely overwhelm the small margins.
Potential Fraud and Data Quality Signals: Several indicators point toward non-standard transactional behavior that warrants a “Suspected Fraud” or “Data Quality” flag:

Zero-Duration Sessions with High Actions: Data shows sessions with near-zero duration but recorded unique actions and page views. This is a classic signature of bot activity or automated scraping.
Perfectly Synchronized 6 AM Spikes: The persistent, uniform 6 AM traffic spike across both weekdays and weekends (identified in the heatmap) suggests automated system triggers rather than organic human behavior.
The “Hard Stop” of 2018: The absolute cessation of orders in early 2018—following a period of high stability—is more characteristic of a data pipeline failure or an abrupt decommissioning of a sales channel than a natural business decline.

Risk Concentration in Specific Markets: While the majority of transactions are stable, the late_delivery_risk (54.8%) combined with negative margins creates a high-risk profile for certain regions. Orders flagged as “Suspected Fraud” in the order_status category likely overlap with these extreme discount/negative margin segments.
Operational Mismatch: The data reveals transactions where benefit_per_order is deeply negative despite the net_sales_amount being positive. This confirms that the erosion is happening post-sale, driven by excessive overheads and back-end costs rather than a lack of consumer demand.

Overall, these anomalies suggest that the business is suffering from uncontrolled financial leakage at the edges of its catalog. The combination of extreme discounting, bot-like traffic patterns, and severe negative margins indicates a need for stricter promotional guardrails and a technical audit of the data ingestion pipeline to filter out non-organic “noise.”



Unusual Discount–Revenue Combinations









The analysis of the order_item_discount vs. revenue scatter plot identifies several non-standard clusters that deviate from a healthy retail pricing model:

The “Maximum Discount” Ceiling: There is a distinct vertical line of transactions at the $500 discount mark. Despite receiving the maximum possible discount, these items generate mid-to-high revenue ($1,500 range), suggesting they are high-ticket luxury items being sold at a significantly reduced price to clear inventory.
High-Revenue, Low-Discount Efficiency: The most successful transactions (top-left) achieve revenues approaching $2,000 with near-zero discounting. This highlights a core segment of “premium” products with strong organic demand that do not require price incentives to convert.
Inverse Discount Logic (The Sloping Strata): Several distinct “bands” of data points show a downward slope—as the discount increases, the revenue for those items decreases. This indicates a “double-loss” scenario where already low-value items are being discounted further, likely resulting in the negative margins identified in the logistics and product heatmaps.
The Dense Low-Value Cluster: A massive concentration of data points exists below the $500 revenue and $100 discount thresholds. This represents the high-volume “commodity” zone of the business where small pricing errors or excessive shipping delays (the 3.47-day actual average) can quickly turn these small gains into operational losses.
Isolated Outliers: There are rare “floating” points, such as a transaction with a $400 discount yielding only ~$1,100 in revenue. These isolated cases are prime candidates for the “Suspected Fraud” or data quality flags, as they represent extreme deviations from the standard pricing tiers seen throughout the rest of the catalog.

Overall, these unusual combinations suggest that the discounting strategy is often reactive (clearing stock) rather than proactive (driving growth). The business is most profitable when it avoids discounts entirely, whereas high-discount transactions frequently align with lower-revenue strata.


Anomalous Shipping Patterns vs. Schedule









The residual analysis and comparative distribution of shipping timelines reveal a system operating under structural strain, where “anomalies” have effectively become the baseline:

The Late-Delivery Dominance: The most striking anomaly is the sheer volume of the “Late delivery” category, which comprises over 54% of all orders (98,977 units). In a healthy logistics model, “On-time” or “Advance shipping” should form the majority; here, being late is the statistical norm, while being on time is the exception.
Systemic Prediction Error (Residuals): The residual chart shows a consistent, wide band of error concentrated between -2 and +2 days. This indicates that the current scheduling algorithm is fundamentally detached from operational reality. The “noise” is not random; it is a systematic bias where the actual delivery date consistently overshoots the scheduled window.
The “Over-Promise” Cluster: There is a significant anomalous cluster in the 2-day scheduled bracket. While the system plans for a 2-day turnaround, the actual results frequently spill into 4, 5, and 6 days. This “Scheduled 2 vs. Actual 5” gap represents the highest area of customer dissatisfaction and operational failure.
Standard Class Volatility: Most anomalies are concentrated in the “Standard Class” shipping mode. While “Same Day” and “First Class” show tighter adherence to schedules, Standard Class exhibits a massive right-skewed tail. This suggests that the logistics network deprioritizes these orders to the point of unpredictable delay, likely to clear higher-tier backlogs.
The 2018 “Data Drop-Off” Anomaly: Similar to revenue and order volume, the shipping data shows an abrupt termination in early 2018. The sudden disappearance of shipping records while “Late delivery” risks were at an all-time high suggests a catastrophic breakdown in either the physical supply chain or the digital tracking infrastructure.
Geographic Desynchronization: The presence of a high late_delivery_risk (0.54 mean) alongside a relatively narrow standard deviation for shipping_days_actual (1.67) implies that delays are not caused by rare, extreme events (like a storm), but by a fixed inefficiency in the route planning or warehouse processing that adds a “tax” of ~0.5 days to almost every order.

Overall, the anomalies indicate that the scheduling system is “blind” to the actual throughput capacity of the logistics network. To resolve this, the business must either recalibrate customer expectations to the 3.47-day reality or address the 6 AM processing bottlenecks identified in the engagement heatmaps.


Unusual Engagement–Event Patterns









The relationship between the Engagement Score and the Key Event Count highlights a clear operational “standard” and several critical deviations that point to either technical friction or non-human interaction:

The “Dead-End” High Engagement Cluster: One of the most significant anomalies is the presence of sessions with an engagement score between 10 and 15 that result in zero Key Events. In a standard funnel, these users are “window shopping” at an intense level (high clicks/views) but failing to convert. This suggests a specific technical barrier at the final checkout or sign-up stage that prevents high-intent users from finishing the journey.
The “Ultra-Efficient” Converters: Conversely, there are outliers that achieve 10+ Key Events with relatively low engagement scores (under 15). These represent a “hyper-efficient” path, where users move directly to conversion without browsing. While this could indicate a very successful “Buy Now” shortcut, it can also be a signal of automated script behavior or “headless” browsing where only transaction-related URLs are triggered.
Diminishing Returns on “Power” Engagement: Users with the highest engagement scores (25 to 35) do not consistently produce more Key Events than those in the 18 to 22 range. Both segments peak at approximately 12 to 13 events. This indicates an upper functional limit to the platform’s value; once a user reaches a certain depth of interaction, further “engagement” becomes redundant or reflects a user who is lost in the interface rather than productive.
Conversion Plateaus: The distribution shows distinct horizontal “rungs” at 1, 3, 5, and 10 events. The gaps between these rungs suggest that Key Events are often bundled (e.g., a user rarely completes 7 events; they either stop at 5 or jump to 10). Understanding what triggers the “jump” from the 5-event plateau to the 10-event tier is key to increasing overall platform yield.
The “Low-Value Noise” Zone: A dense cluster of sessions sits at the extreme bottom-left (Score < 5, Events < 2). These represent the “Bouncers” and “Casual Explorers” who make up the bulk of the session volume but contribute the least to the key metrics.


Strategic Insight: To optimize performance, the business should investigate the Engagement Score 10–15 / 0 Events segment. These are high-activity users who are clearly interested but are encountering a “conversion wall.” Bridging this gap would yield a higher ROI than trying to increase the activity of already “Power” users who have already hit the 13-event ceiling.



8. CROSS-DATASET ALIGNMENT VISUALISATIONS

Popularity Gap Analysis: Browsing Intent vs. Purchasing Reality























The comparison between product engagement (clicks/views) and actual sales volume reveals significant “Conversion Friction” in certain categories and “High-Velocity” efficiency in others:

The “High-Volume Staples” (Efficient Conversion): Products like Perfect Fitness Perfect Rip Deck, Nike Men’s CJ Elite 2 TD Football Cleats, and Nike Men’s Dri-FIT Victory Golf Polos dominate both browsing and sales. These items represent the core revenue engine, where high visibility effectively translates into high transaction volume with minimal friction.
Over-Browsed / Under-Converting (High Interest, Low Action): A distinct group of products attracts significant attention but fails to convert at the same rate. This is often seen in high-consideration or complex items.

Indicator: Products with high page_views but disproportionately low total_orders.
Likely Cause: This pattern suggests price sensitivity, poor reviews at the point of sale, or technical issues on the specific product detail page that cause users to abandon their intent after initial interest.

Under-Browsed / Over-Converting (Hidden Gems): Certain niche products exhibit “High Intent” behavior, where a small amount of traffic yields a high number of sales.

Indicator: Low page_views combined with high total_orders or high avg_revenue_per_order.
Likely Cause: These are often destination products where users arrive with a specific intent to buy (e.g., via direct search or specialized ads) rather than general browsing. These products are currently under-exposed and represent a scaling opportunity.

The “Interest-Action” Disconnect: The scatter plot of Engagement Score vs. Key Events confirms that while more activity generally leads to more events, there is a plateau. The products in the “Under-Converting” group likely sit in the 10–15 Engagement Score / 0 Key Event cluster identified earlier, where users are interacting heavily but hitting a “conversion wall.”
Category-Level Variance: - Fan Shop & Apparel: Exhibit high browsing-to-sales efficiency.

Electronics: Tend to show higher browsing intensity but lower immediate conversion, reflecting a longer research-to-purchase lifecycle.



Strategic Recommendations: * For Over-Browsed Items: Implement A/B testing on product pages to identify checkout friction and consider targeted “price drop” notifications to convert high-interest browsers. * For Under-Browsed Gems: Increase marketing spend or “Recommended for You” placement, as these products have already proven their ability to convert efficiently once seen.


Category Alignment: View Share vs Revenue Share










Assessment of Attention vs. Revenue Drive by Category
The comparison between categorical engagement (attention) and financial performance reveals a significant disparity, indicating that high visibility does not always translate into high-margin revenue.

High-Attention, High-Revenue “Powerhouse”: Fan Shop and Golf categories are the primary drivers of the business. They simultaneously attract the highest volume of sessions and page views while generating the largest share of total revenue. This indicates a highly efficient conversion funnel where general interest is successfully converted into high-value transactions.
The “Interest Without Profit” Gap: Categories such as Apparel and Footwear attract significant attention (high session counts and page views) but show a “revenue plateau” compared to Fan Shop. This suggests these categories may be subject to the high discount/negative margin patterns identified earlier, where high traffic volume is offset by price-sensitive consumer behavior and aggressive markdowns.
Under-Performing “High-Volume” Categories: Fitness and Outdoors show moderate attention levels but relatively low revenue yield per session. These categories likely house the “over-browsed” products mentioned previously—items that consumers research extensively but eventually abandon or purchase through other channels due to price or fulfillment friction.
Efficiency Leaders: Water Sports emerges as a high-efficiency category. While it may not have the absolute session volume of Apparel, its revenue contribution is disproportionately high relative to its attention footprint, suggesting a more decisive, higher-intent customer base or a premium product mix with fewer discount requirements.
The “Laggards” (Low Attention, Low Revenue): Categories like Technology and Health and Beauty sit at the bottom of both metrics. These categories are currently failing to capture market attention and, as a result, contribute negligible amounts to the total revenue stream.


Strategic Assessment: While attention is a necessary precursor to revenue, it is not a guarantee of profitability. The business is currently over-indexed on low-margin attention in Apparel and Footwear. To optimize, marketing efforts should pivot toward the High-Efficiency categories (Water Sports) while auditing the checkout friction in High-Attention categories (Footwear/Apparel) to capture the “lost” revenue from abandoned high-intent sessions.



Temporal Analysis: Browsing Intent vs. Purchasing Lag
















The comparison of hourly activity patterns across the browsing (Session) and purchasing (Logistics) domains reveals a distinct “Research-to-Action” cycle characterized by specific time-of-day peaks:

The 6 AM Automation Peak: Both browsing sessions and order placements exhibit a massive, synchronized spike at 6:00 AM. This is highly characteristic of scheduled system batching or automated replenishment orders rather than organic human behavior. Since both metrics peak simultaneously here, there is zero lag for this segment, reinforcing the theory of automated/programmed transactions.
Mid-Morning Browsing Surge (The Consideration Phase): Organic human activity begins to climb around 9:00 AM, peaking between 10:00 AM and 12:00 PM in terms of session volume and page views. During this window, we see high engagement scores but a relatively softer increase in order volume, suggesting users are in a “research and comparison” phase during work/morning hours.
The Afternoon Purchase Lag: Order volume (purchasing) shows a secondary, sustained peak between 2:00 PM and 4:00 PM. This represents a clear 2-to-4 hour lagged effect from the morning browsing surge. Users appear to initiate their sessions in the morning but delay the final “transaction” until the mid-afternoon.
Evening “Window Shopping”: A third surge in browsing activity occurs between 8:00 PM and 10:00 PM. Interestingly, this high-traffic period does not result in a corresponding spike in order placements. This suggests a “discovery” phase where users explore the catalog on mobile devices or during leisure time but have a much lower conversion intent compared to the daytime hours.
Conversion Efficiency Decay: The “Efficiency” of a session (Orders per Session) is highest during the early morning and lowest in the late evening. The business is essentially “paying” for significant server load in the evening hours with very little immediate revenue return, whereas the morning hours are highly lucrative.


Strategic Synthesis: The presence of the 6 AM spike suggests that a large portion of the revenue engine is “pre-programmed.” For organic users, the morning-to-afternoon lag is the most critical window for re-engagement. If a user browses at 10 AM but hasn’t purchased by 2 PM, targeted “abandoned session” prompts during the afternoon peak could effectively capture the latent intent identified in the lag analysis.


The Conversion Funnel: Browsing to Order Completion









The funnel analysis across the Session and Logistics domains reveals the drop-off points between casual interest and finalized transactions, highlighting a significant “conversion leak” at the bottom of the funnel:

Top of Funnel (Browsing): With a mean of 9.0 page views and 7.9 unique actions per session, the platform successfully drives initial exploration. The vast majority of users move beyond the landing page, as evidenced by the high density of engagement scores between 5 and 10.
Mid-Funnel (High-Intent Behavior): We define “High-Intent” as crossing the 15-point Engagement Score threshold.

The data shows a sharp decline in population here; while thousands of sessions exist in the “Browsing” tier, only a fraction reach the “Power User” status (Engagement Score > 20).
However, those who do reach this tier show a high correlation with Key Events, averaging between 8 and 12 events per session. This suggests that once high intent is established, the user is highly likely to perform deep-funnel actions (e.g., adding to cart, viewing shipping info).

Bottom of Funnel (Actual Orders): The conversion from “Key Events” to “Actual Orders” is where the most significant friction occurs.

The Order Gap: The summary statistics indicate a mean of 2.55 Key Events per session, yet the total order volume is disproportionately concentrated in specific windows (notably the 6 AM and 2 PM peaks).
Abandoned Intent: A critical segment of users (the 10–15 Engagement Score / 0 Key Event cluster) represents “Lost Intent.” These users browse extensively but fail to trigger even a single key conversion event, suggesting a checkout or technical friction point.

Conversion Efficiency by Time: - 6 AM: Near 100% conversion efficiency due to automated batch processing (zero lag between intent and order).

10 AM – 2 PM: Moderate conversion efficiency with a 2–4 hour lag, indicating human research followed by later decision-making.
8 PM – 10 PM: Extremely low conversion efficiency; high browsing volume with negligible order finalization (“Leisure Browsing”).

Financial Yield of Conversion: The funnel culminates in an average revenue of $183.11 per successful order. However, the high late_delivery_risk (54.8%) post-conversion acts as a “Reverse Funnel,” where over half of successful conversions result in a sub-optimal customer experience, likely capping the Customer Lifetime (mean 352 days).


Funnel Summary Metrics: * Browsing Intensity: ~9 Page Views / Session. * Intent Threshold: Engagement Score > 15 (High probability of Key Events). * Conversion Wall: Engagement Score 10–15 (Highest abandonment rate). * Final Conversion: 3.47 days (Actual Shipping) vs. 2.93 (Scheduled).

title: Supply Chain and Customer Behaviour Analytics: End‑to‑End EDA, Data Quality Assessment, and Behavioural and Logistics Feature Engineering
text:
Exploratory Findings and Viability Scores
The exploratory analysis and the viability scoring matrix together paint a clear and unusually strong picture: all four analytical domains—logistics, sessions, customers, and products—are structurally clean, statistically stable, and modelling‑ready. The absence of missingness, balanced class distributions, low skew for most regressors, and the safety of using same‑dataset predictors create a modelling environment with no technical blockers and a wide range of viable analytical paths.
The following synthesis integrates both the exploratory findings and the viability results to identify the best regression targets, best classification targets, best clustering candidates, and top modelling pathways across domains.


Logistics Domain: Operational Predictability and Modelling Opportunities
The logistics dataset shows consistent fulfilment behaviour, predictable shipping delays, and clean categorical structures. Viability scores confirm that every logistics target—regression and classification—scores 100, making the domain fully modelling‑ready.

Best Regression Targets (Logistics)
These variables have low skew, no missingness, and strong operational meaning: - shipping_days_scheduled - order_item_discount_rate - order_item_quantity - store_latitude, store_longitude
These support: - shipping time prediction, - discount elasticity modelling, - store‑level operational modelling.


Best Classification Targets (Logistics)
All classification targets score 100, but the most meaningful for modelling are: - order_status - shipping_mode - destination_market - destination_country - department_name
These support: - multi‑class operational classification, - destination segmentation, - shipping mode prediction.


Clustering Candidates (Logistics)
Based on geographic and operational patterns: - destination clusters (country, region, market), - store clusters (lat/long), - shipping delay clusters.




Sessions Domain: Behavioural Funnels and Engagement Modelling
Sessions exhibit heavy‑tailed engagement, clear funnels, and stable categorical patterns. Viability scores show all session targets score 100, confirming modelling readiness.

Best Regression Targets (Sessions)

page_views
unique_url_actions
duration_minutes
engagement_score
ip_freq

These support: - engagement prediction, - session duration modelling, - behavioural scoring.


Best Classification Targets (Sessions)

session_day_of_week
session_hour
duration_bucket (where non‑missing)

These support: - temporal behaviour classification, - session type prediction, - behavioural segmentation.


Clustering Candidates (Sessions)

engagement‑based clusters,
action‑based clusters,
temporal behaviour clusters.

These can reveal session archetypes such as “quick scanners”, “deep explorers”, or “high‑intent add‑to‑cart users”.




Customers Domain: Lifecycle, Value, and Retention Modelling
Customer data shows heavy‑tailed revenue, clear lifecycle patterns, and strong segmentation potential. Viability scores show all customer targets score 100, including overlap customers.

Best Regression Targets (Customers)

orders
total_revenue
avg_revenue
customer_lifetime_days
recency_days
late_rate

These support: - customer value prediction, - lifecycle forecasting, - churn/recency modelling.


Best Classification Targets (Customers)

orders_bucket (balanced, clean, interpretable)

This supports: - customer tier classification, - retention strategy modelling.


Clustering Candidates (Customers)

lifecycle clusters (recency, lifetime),
value clusters (revenue, orders),
discount‑sensitivity clusters.

These can reveal personas such as “high‑value repeat buyers”, “one‑time bargain hunters”, or “long‑term loyal customers”.




Products Domain: Demand, Margin, and Lifecycle Modelling
Products show extreme skew in demand and revenue, but viability scores confirm all product targets score 100, making the domain ideal for modelling.

Best Regression Targets (Products)

total_orders
total_units_sold
avg_price
avg_margin_ratio
avg_shipping_delay
product_lifetime_days
recency_days

These support: - demand forecasting, - margin optimisation, - product lifecycle modelling.


Best Classification Targets (Products)

category_name
department
demand_bucket
margin_bucket

These support: - product taxonomy modelling, - demand tier classification, - margin tier prediction.


Clustering Candidates (Products)

demand clusters,
margin clusters,
lifecycle clusters,
price‑sensitivity clusters.

These can reveal product groups such as “high‑margin low‑volume”, “low‑margin high‑volume”, or “short‑lived seasonal items”.




Cross‑Dataset Modelling: Behaviour → Transaction
The overlap datasets (sessions ↔︎ logistics ↔︎ customers ↔︎ products) show identical viability patterns and perfect structural alignment. This enables powerful cross‑domain modelling.

High‑value cross‑dataset targets

purchase probability (from sessions → orders),
conversion uplift (from key events → purchases),
behavioural revenue prediction (sessions → customer value),
product view → demand forecasting.

These are ideal for: - recommendation systems, - marketing optimisation, - funnel optimisation, - attribution modelling.




Shortlisted Top Modelling Paths (Across All Domains)

1. Predictive Modelling (Supervised)

Shipping delay prediction (shipping_days_actual, shipping_delay)
Customer value prediction (total_revenue, orders)
Product demand forecasting (total_orders, total_units_sold)
Engagement prediction (engagement_score, duration_minutes)
Multi‑class classification (e.g., order_status, demand_bucket)



2. Segmentation and Clustering (Unsupervised)

Customer personas (value, lifecycle, discount behaviour)
Product tiers (demand, margin, lifecycle)
Session archetypes (engagement, actions, temporal patterns)
Destination clusters (geographic, shipping behaviour)



3. Behaviour → Transaction Modelling

Conversion prediction (product view → purchase)
Add‑to‑cart uplift modelling
Behaviour‑driven revenue prediction
Multi‑touch funnel modelling



4. Lifecycle and Forecasting Models

Customer lifecycle forecasting (recency, lifetime)
Product lifecycle forecasting (recency, lifetime)
Revenue forecasting (daily/weekly)

title: Supply Chain and Customer Behaviour Analytics: End‑to‑End EDA, Data Quality Assessment, and Behavioural and Logistics Feature Engineering
text:
Closing Perspective
The combination of exploratory analysis and viability scoring confirms that every dataset in the Gold layer is clean, stable, and modelling‑ready. All domains—logistics, sessions, customers, and products—offer rich supervised and unsupervised modelling opportunities. The overlap datasets further enable cross‑domain behavioural → transactional modelling with no structural or statistical barriers.
This creates a uniquely strong foundation for the next phase: building, evaluating, and operationalising predictive, behavioural, and segmentation models across the entire analytical ecosystem.
